issues.title: Create an ChatProcessor
issues.author: Marcus_Johansson
issues.description:
With https://git.drupalcode.org/project/ai/-/work_items/3492940 the agents could create ChatProcessor that has the settings the AI Assistant layer has.

Discussion has to be made if we already support multiple threads and want that in the future, so you can basically have one chat where you can pick the agent you talk to. But for 1-to-1 functionality parity, it means that the consumer sets one agent per chatbot.

### Proposed resolution

Create a ChatProcessor for agent that has a form that sets:\
\* Agent\
\* Specific Error Messages (?)\
\* Allow History (?)

We need to discuss how we store history in general, but that could potentially be set by the ChatProcessor.

issues.title: Add the possibility to turn off url whitelisting
issues.author: Marcus_Johansson
issues.description:
The security issue https://www.drupal.org/sa-contrib-2026-028 did introduce a whitelist of hostnames for anything the AI produces. Since it was deemed a security issue we di a widecatching solution that means that any url anywhere is catched, including things that might look similar to the urls.

With agents you might want to have the possibility to actually turn this off, for a multitude of reasons - including that you know that the output is safe or that the agent you are using does never have any context that is classified.

This means that we should add the possibility to turn this off.

### Solution

1. Add a checkbox to the agent form and modeler to bypass the url whitelist
2. Add it to the agents config and schema
3. If its checked, it should reset the mode programatically, see: https://project.pages.drupalcode.org/ai/1.3.x/developers/hostname_filter/#programmatic-configuration-for-testing
4. Write tests, to make sure that this works

issues.title: [Meta] Create AI CMS Demo System
issues.author: drupalbot
issues.description:
>>> [!note] Migrated issue Reported by: [breidert](https://www.drupal.org/user/135619) >>>

[Tracker]
Update Summary: [One-line status update for stakeholders]
Check-in Date: MM/DD/YYYY
Additional Collaborators:
Metadata is used by the AI Tracker. Docs and additional fields here.
[/Tracker]

Summary

This meta issue covers the creation of a CMS Demo System that showcases the latest curated AI functionality developed during the Product Development Sprints. The system serves as a living reference implementation — always reflecting the current state of AI feature development — and provides a foundation for hosting-specific demo experiences built on top of it.

Architecture

The Demo System is maintained as a standalone project in GitLab with the following branch structure:

  • main: Contains the assembled AI functionality as developed in the Product Development Sprints. This is the canonical reference and is publicly accessible.
  • Hoster branches: Branched off main, these contain hosting-platform-specific adjustments to models, configurations, and capabilities. Initial target platforms include Amazee.io, Dropsolid, Pantheon, and Acquia Cloud. Each platform may require different AI model configurations or feature toggles to reflect their specific capabilities.
  • demo: A dedicated branch for local development, allowing contributors and partners to spin up the full demo experience using DDEV or a similar local development tool.

Access Model

Access to this repository is restricted to Drupal AI Partners (Makers). This applies to the demo setup itself — the configuration, hosting-specific customizations, and the work of assembling the AI features into a cohesive demo experience.

All underlying source code and AI functionality developed during the Product Development Sprints remains publicly available as before. What is restricted is not the software itself, but the setup that makes it work together as a demo, and the hoster-specific adaptations built on top of it. Makers can collaborate across all branches and share access to the DDEV-based local demo setup.

Out of Scope

The display of launch buttons or embedded demo widgets on Drupal.org is explicitly out of scope for this issue. However, the setup created here can serve as the technical foundation for existing or future demo experiences published on Drupal.org.

Goals for the First Iteration

  • Technical foundation: Establish the GitLab project structure, define branching conventions, and document the contribution workflow for both the main branch and hoster-specific branches.
  • Team formation: Bring together a team of Drupal AI Partners responsible for managing and maintaining the demo experiences over time.
  • First POC: If capacity allows, produce a first proof-of-concept demo experience — either via the DDEV-based demo branch or one hosting-specific branch — demonstrating end-to-end functionality of a curated set of AI features.

AI usage (if applicable)

[x] AI Assisted Issue
This issue was generated with AI assistance, but was reviewed and refined by the creator.

[ ] AI Assisted Code
This code was mainly generated by a human, with AI autocompleting or parts AI generated, but under full human supervision.

[ ] AI Generated Code
This code was mainly generated by an AI with human guidance, and reviewed, tested, and refined by a human.

[ ] Vibe Coded
This code was generated by an AI and has only been functionally tested.

comments.author: kepol
comments.body:
> _Migrated from comment #4 posted 15 Apr 2026 at 03:50 UTC_

Jamie provided notes in February via Slack, and maybe had a doc somewhere, but I can't find it.

Add someone here. (link to the old contributor tab on the sponsorship sheet)

  • Need their drupal.org username as well as name (careful that sometimes the URL says - but their username has a space, the API cares about that).
  • Needs the Organisation name and the machine name for their organisation on Drupal.org (find it in the URL).
  • Whether they are "Developer" or "Organisational" is important.
  • Whether they are a maker or not.
  • I tend to put all the makers nearer the top but the order doesn't matter
  • IN the appear in dashboard write "yes" like the others.
  • Other things don't matter as much.

Go to the Contributors CSV export tab.

  • Highlight the second row and copy it down all the way to the bottom until you are sure you've got everyone (moving things around on the other sheet messes with this).
  • File - Download -> CSV.
  • Do NOT open the CSV file. Opening it and saving it can save loads of random stuff depending on operatiing systeming messing with people with letters like umlaut.

Go to starforge and login as an admin: https://www.drupalstarforge.ai/user/

Go to Ai -> Import Contributors

  • https://www.drupalstarforge.ai/ai-dashboard/admin/contributor-import
  • Under Upload CSV file upload the downloaded CSV file and then click Import Contributors in the top right.

issues.title: Incorrect Content and Links on AI Providers Page
issues.author: drupalbot
issues.description:
>>> [!note] Migrated issue Reported by: [afoster](https://www.drupal.org/user/594458) >>>

Summary

The AI Providers page contains several content mix-ups where provider descriptions and "More about..." links are incorrectly matched to the wrong providers.

Page URL

https://new.drupal.org/ai/about-drupal-ai/providers

Issues Found

1. DeepL Translate Section — Wrong Description and Link

Current (Incorrect):

  • Description: "ElevenLabs is a text-to-speech service that can create speech from any text, even using custom trained voices..."
  • Link text: "More about ElevenLabs Translate AI provider"

Expected (Correct):

  • Description: Should describe DeepL as an AI-powered translation service (e.g., "DeepL is an AI-powered translation service that provides highly accurate, context-aware translations. The module serves as a DeepL provider for the AI module, integrating the DeepL text translation API through Drupal's AI abstraction layer.")
  • Link text: "More about DeepL AI provider"
  • Link URL: https://www.drupal.org/project/ai_provider_deepl

2. ElevenLabs Section — Incorrect Link Text

Current (Incorrect):

  • Link text: "More about ElevenLabs Translate AI provider"

Expected (Correct):

  • Link text: "More about ElevenLabs AI provider" (remove errant "Translate")
  • Link URL: https://www.drupal.org/project/elevenlabs

Note: The description for ElevenLabs is correct, only the link text contains an error.

3. Fireworks AI Section — Wrong Description and Link

Current (Incorrect):

  • Description: "Google Gemini is an advanced AI from Google that can understand and generate text, images, and code..."
  • Link text: "More about Google Gemini provider"

Expected (Correct):

  • Description: Should describe Fireworks AI (e.g., "Fireworks AI is a provider for the AI module, enabling integration with various AI models through the AI abstraction layer. It supports models such as Stable Diffusion XL, LLama 3, and FireLlava, offering cost-effective alternatives for AI workflows.")
  • Link text: "More about Fireworks AI provider"
  • Link URL: https://www.drupal.org/project/fireworksai

Steps to Reproduce

  1. Visit https://new.drupal.org/ai/about-drupal-ai/providers
  2. Scroll to the "DeepL Translate" section — observe description mentions ElevenLabs
  3. Scroll to the "ElevenLabs" section — observe link text says "ElevenLabs Translate"
  4. Scroll to the "Fireworks AI" section — observe description mentions Google Gemini

Correct Module URLs for Reference

Provider Correct Project URL
DeepL https://www.drupal.org/project/ai_provider_deepl
ElevenLabs https://www.drupal.org/project/elevenlabs
Fireworks AI https://www.drupal.org/project/fireworksai

comments.body:
> _Migrated from comment #3 posted 10 Jul 2025 at 16:28 UTC_ ``` - Assigned: breidert + Assigned: kristen pol ```

@kristen I would suggest the following:

Bug

Title

Clear, concise summary of the problem (e.g., "Crash on login when username is blank")

Description

- What’s happening vs. what’s expected?
- Include any error messages, stack traces, or logs.
- If it’s a regression, mention when it last worked.
- Describe expected behavior

Steps to Reproduce in Drupal AI DDEV environment

1. Go to ‘...’
2. Click on ‘...’
3. See error

Environment

- Drupal version:
- Drupal AI version:
- Browser (if relevant):
- Any relevant config or flags

Todos

- Fix the bug
- Provide testing instructions in Drupal AI DDEV environment

Requirements / Acceptance Criteria

- When I follow the testing instructions I can verify the feature works

URLs (if applicable)

- Link to the affected page (can be relative)

Screenshots (if applicable)

- Provide a screenshot showing the issue

Feature

Title

Short summary of the feature (e.g., "Add dark mode toggle in settings")

Description

- What is the feature?
- What problem does it solve or what value does it add?

User Story / Use Case

As a [type of user], I want to [goal] so that [benefit].

Todos

- Create concept
- Implement
- Provide testing instructions in Drupal AI DDEV environment
- Provide documentation

Requirements / Acceptance Criteria

- When I read the documentation I understand how the feature is supposed to work
- When I follow the testing instructions I can verify the feature works

Design / UI Notes

- Link to mockups, wireframes, or design files if available

Dependencies or Related Work

- Mention any backend work, third-party libraries, or affected components

URLs (if applicable)

- Link to spec, design, or existing feature area

Screenshots (if applicable)

- Add mockups, visual references, or examples

Task

Task Requirements

Title

Describe the task clearly (e.g., "Provide documentation for new contributors")

Description

- What needs to be done and why
- Not necessarily user-facing

Checklist / Subtasks

- Break down into actionable steps if helpful

Context / Background

- Link to related tickets, technical docs, Slack threads, etc.

Todos

- Describe what needs to be done

Requirements / Acceptance Criteria

- Describe what does “finished” looks like

> _Migrated from comment #9 posted 15 Jan 2026 at 15:57 UTC_

I agree that providing such a long template with different request templates all in same template might be overwhelming, specially for someone who is unfamiliar with the process and just wants to report something they see / would like to see.

The 3 types of issue already contain shared sections, title, description, todos, requirements, URL/screenshots.

I reckon just like the current template includes "Optional" sections, <h3>Optional: Other details as applicable....</h3> it can be the case here that we have a single template, with sections labeled as required / optional depending on what type of issue the user is writing about. It also gives an idea of what they can be expected to write about. Rough idea:

<h3 id="summary-problem-motivation">Problem / Motivation</h3>
<p><em><strong>Required for all issues.</strong></em></p>

<h3 id="summary-steps-reproduce">Steps to reproduce</h3>
<p><em><strong>Required for bugs. Optional otherwise.</strong></em></p>

<h3 id="summary-expected-behavior">Expected behavior</h3>
<p><em><strong>Required for bugs. Recommended for features.</strong></em></p>

<h3 id="summary-feature-details">Feature details / Use case</h3>
<p><em><strong>Recommended for feature requests. Optional otherwise.</strong></em></p>

<h3 id="summary-environment">Environment</h3>
<p><em><strong>Required for bugs. Optional otherwise.</strong></em></p>
<ul>
  <li>Drupal core version:</li>
  <li>Drupal AI module version:</li>
  <li>...</li>
</ul>

<h3 id="summary-proposed-resolution">Proposed resolution</h3>
<p><em><strong>Optional for all issues.</strong></em></p>

<h3 id="summary-remaining-tasks">Remaining tasks / Todos</h3>
<p><em><strong>Optional but encouraged.</strong></em></p>

<h3 id="summary-acceptance-criteria">Requirements / Acceptance criteria</h3>
<p><em><strong>Recommended for features and tasks.</strong></em></p>

issues.title: Create responsible LLM use training, guidance, and tools package for Drupal contributors
issues.author: drupalbot
issues.description:
>>> [!note] Migrated issue Reported by: [scott falconer](https://www.drupal.org/user/52557) >>>

This issue was spun off from #3574093: Ban slop issue summaries and comments to focus only on responsible LLM use training and guidance for Drupal contributors.

Please keep discussion here scoped to training, enablement, and tools. This issue should build on policy decisions as documented in Policy on the use of AI when contributing to Drupal.

Problem/Motivation
As Drupal allows some forms of AI-assisted contribution as defined in Policy on the use of AI when contributing to Drupal, contributors need practical training and tools to use these capabilities responsibly, and without shifting the review burden onto other contributors, or negatively impacting those who choose not the use LLMs.

Existing contributor and review-guide work is helpful, but there is still room for a more explicit training path focused on responsible LLM use in Drupal contribution workflows.

Proposed resolution
Create training/guidance materials and tools for responsible LLM use in Drupal contribution workflows.

Possible topics:

  • Understanding the issue and recent discussion before prompting
  • Disclosure expectations
  • Local testing and verification
  • Explaining generated output during review
  • Revising code without re-dumping AI output
  • Respecting maintainer and reviewer time
  • When not to use AI
  • Mentorship / onboarding implications

Possible outputs:

  • contributor documentation
  • AI generation best practices
  • sample AGENTS.md files, Agent skills, and tooling
  • workshop/session materials
  • checklist or playbook
  • Dependencies

AI disclosure, the above was researched and discussed with AI prior to posting, it was then reviewed and drafted by humans.

issues.title: [Meta] Move Issue Management of AI projects to Gitlab
issues.author: drupalbot
issues.description:
>>> [!note] Migrated issue Reported by: [breidert](https://www.drupal.org/user/135619) >>>

[Tracker]
Update Summary: [One-line status update for stakeholders]
Check-in Date: MM/DD/YYYY
Additional Collaborators:
Metadata is used by the AI Tracker. Docs and additional fields here.
[/Tracker]

Summary

This meta issue tracks the preparation and execution of migrating the AI Initiative projects from Drupal.org's legacy issue queue to GitLab. The goal is to align workflows, prepare projects, and coordinate the migration with all project managers — ideally completing preparation within the current sprint and executing the migration around DrupalCon Chicago.

Background

A planning meeting was held with the Drupal.org infrastructure team to review the migration process in detail. The process was walked through end-to-end, existing migrated projects were examined as reference points (e.g. https://git.drupalcode.org/project/gitlab_templates/-/issues), and no blockers were identified. The migration can proceed once timing is agreed upon.

Migration process

Migrating a project to GitLab is straightforward: it is triggered by running a single Drush command and takes approximately 1 hour per project. Once migrated, the project's issues and merge requests are managed entirely within GitLab.

Contribution credits

Drupal.org contribution credits continue to work after migration. A bot monitors GitLab issues and automatically posts comments linking back to Drupal.org contribution records, so attribution can be assigned in the usual way. Example: https://git.drupalcode.org/project/gitlab_templates/-/issues/3572376#note_710310

Issue management

Issues can be viewed both at the individual project level and at the group level, which provides a unified view across all projects in the AI Initiative. This makes it possible to manage sprints, filter by label, and track work across modules in a single board — without leaving GitLab. Full GitLab project management functionality is available, including milestones, labels, issue boards, epics, and merge request workflows. Example group-level view: https://git.drupalcode.org/groups/project/-/issues?sort=created_date&state=opened&label_name%5B%5D=state%3A%3Artbc&first_page_size=20

Timing is dependent on Drupal.org infrastructure team availability to respond quickly if anything requires attention post-migration. The earliest realistic target is DrupalCon Chicago.

Projects to migrate

  • https://www.drupal.org/project/ai
  • https://www.drupal.org/project/ai_initiative
  • https://www.drupal.org/project/ai_dasboard
  • https://www.drupal.org/project/ai_recipe_image_classification
  • https://www.drupal.org/project/ai_recipe_content_classification
  • https://www.drupal.org/project/tool
  • https://www.drupal.org/project/field_widget_actions
  • https://www.drupal.org/project/ai_agents
  • https://www.drupal.org/project/ai_context
  • https://www.drupal.org/project/canvas
  • https://www.drupal.org/project/flowdrop_ui_agents

Preparation tasks

1. Project selection & timing

  • Confirm final list of projects to migrate in this round
  • Agree on migration window (target: DrupalCon Chicago or shortly before)
  • Coordinate with Drupal.org infrastructure team to confirm availability during migration

2. Project manager communication

  • Notify all project managers of affected projects
  • Align on expectations: workflows, issue management, MR templates
  • Get sign-off from all stakeholders

3. Prepare each project

  • Audit and remove unnecessary labels from existing issue queues
  • Prepare issue templates (bug report, feature request, etc.)
  • Prepare merge request (MR) templates

4. Define new workflows

  • Set up issue boards (sprint view, backlog, etc.)
  • Align on label taxonomy across projects
  • Define milestone/sprint conventions

5. Documentation

  • Document the migration process and new workflows
  • Suggested location: AI Initiative project GitLab Documentation section
  • Include onboarding notes for contributors unfamiliar with GitLab issue queues

6. Execute migration

  • Run Drush migration command per project (~1 hour each)
  • Verify contribution credit bot is active on each migrated project
  • Confirm group-level issue views are working correctly

Proposed next steps

  1. Workshop (~2 hours) with Drupal AI project managers to align, divide tasks, and finalize preparation plan
  2. Complete all preparation tasks within the current sprint
  3. Align with remaining stakeholders after the workshop
  4. Execute migration around DrupalCon Chicago

This issue was generated with AI assistance, but was reviewed and refined by the creator.

[ ] AI Assisted Code
This code was mainly generated by a human, with AI autocompleting or parts AI generated, but under full human supervision.

[ ] AI Generated Code
This code was mainly generated by an AI with human guidance, and reviewed, tested, and refined by a human.

[ ] Vibe Coded

issues.title: [Meta] AI Prompt Safety Guardrails Recipe
issues.author: drupalbot
issues.description:
>>> [!note] Migrated issue Reported by: [breidert](https://www.drupal.org/user/135619) >>>

[Tracker]
Update Summary: [One-line status update for stakeholders]
Check-in Date: MM/DD/YYYY
Additional Collaborators:
Metadata is used by the AI Tracker. Docs and additional fields here.
[/Tracker]

Overview

This meta issue covers the creation of a Drupal Recipe (ai_recipe_guardrails_prompt_safety) that ships sensible default guardrail configuration for protecting AI interactions against prompt attacks and off-topic or high-liability content.

The recipe builds on the guardrail plugin system introduced in #3518963 and combines two complementary guardrail types:

  • Regex guardrails for detecting structurally identifiable attack patterns — deterministic, zero LLM cost, negligible latency.
  • Topic guardrails for detecting the intent behind a prompt — using AI classification to catch semantic attacks and off-topic or high-liability content that no regex pattern could reliably identify.

Once the recipe is installed, AI interactions are immediately protected against the most common categories of adversarial input and liability-relevant content, without requiring any configuration.

Use Cases

Structural attack detection (regex)

  • Script and code injection: A user embeds executable code or script tags in their input in an attempt to manipulate how the output is rendered or processed downstream — particularly relevant when AI output is written into content fields or rendered in a browser context. Regex detection is reliable and cost-free for these
    structurally well-defined patterns.

Semantic prompt attack detection (topic filter)

Jailbreak attempts and prompt injection attacks are constantly evolving in their phrasing. A regex pattern cannot reliably keep up with creative variations such as indirect framings, roleplay setups, or obfuscated instructions. A topic-based guardrail — which has an AI classify the intent of the input against a list of known attack categories — is significantly more robust.

  • Prompt injection and system prompt override: The user attempts to instruct the AI to ignore, forget, or override its configured system prompt and instructions.
  • Role hijacking: The user attempts to redefine the AI's identity or role — for example by claiming it is now a different assistant with different rules, or that a special unrestricted mode has
    been activated.
  • Jailbreak attempts: Common patterns used to circumvent AI safety instructions, such as hypothetical framings, roleplay scenarios designed to bypass guardrails, or requests to behave
    as an AI without restrictions.

Off-topic and liability content (topic filter)

Many sites need to ensure their AI interactions stay within a defined scope and do not venture into areas that carry legal or reputational risk.

The following topic guardrails are broadly applicable regardless of site type and are included as ready-to-use defaults:

  • Medical advice block: Prevents the AI from responding to requests for medical diagnosis, treatment recommendations, or medication guidance. Relevant for almost any public-facing AI
    interaction.
  • Legal advice block: Prevents the AI from providing legal opinions, contract interpretation, or litigation guidance.
  • Sensitive topics block: Prevents the AI from engaging with socially divisive or politically charged subjects where any response carries reputational risk for the site owner. Covered topics include politics, race, religion, war, ethnicity, immigration, abortion, gun control, capital punishment, euthanasia, terrorism, genocide, sexual orientation, gender identity, drug legalisation, extremism, and human trafficking.

Testing instructions

Setup Drupal

# Create and configure project
mkdir drupal-ai-dev
cd drupal-ai-dev/
ddev config --project-type=drupal11 --docroot=web
ddev start
# Install Drupal
ddev composer create-project "drupal/recommended-project:^11"
ddev composer require drush/drush
ddev drush site:install --account-name=admin --account-pass=admin -y
composer require 'drupal/ai:^1.3@RC'
ddev composer require 'drupal/ai:^1.3@RC'
ddev composer require drupal/ai_provider_openai
ddev drush cr
ddev drush en ai_provider_openai -y

Clone and install recipe

mkdir recipes
cd recipes
git clone git@git.drupal.org:project/ai_recipe_guardrails_prompt_safety.git
ddev drush recipe ../recipes/ai_recipe_guardrails_prompt_safety

Test guardrails

comments.author: abhisekmazumdar
comments.body:
> _Migrated from comment #10 posted 12 Mar 2026 at 16:52 UTC_ ``` - Status: Needs review + Status: Needs work ```

Hi
Yes this also cooks well.

But few points to address are:

1. The same dependencies declared for the Guardrails sets

Its a ai module bug: #3578846: AiGuardrailSet does not declare config dependencies on its referenced guardrails

2. Case-sensitive topic matching in RestrictToTopic

Again ai module bug I feel
In RestrictToTopic.php:291-298:

if (\in_array($topic, $valid_topics)) {   // strict, case-sensitive

The LLM is asked to return which topics are present, but the comparison against configured topics is exact/case-sensitive. If the recipe configures "politics" and the LLM returns "Politics", the invalid topic is silently missed. This affects all four restrict_to_topic guardrails in this recipe. The fix needs to be in the AI module, but it's worth flagging as a known gap. I will report a bug there for this as well.
Open a bug: #3579079: Restrict Topic guardrail silently bypassed due to case-sensitive topic matching

3. Potential false positive: Security: HTML Event Handler Injection

The pattern /\bon\w+\s*=/i is intended to catch onerror=, onclick=, etc. But \bon\w+ also matches any word starting with "on" followed by letters, such as:

online= (URL query parameter)
once= (valid in some config or CSS contexts)
onto=

Maybe we can consider anchoring more tightly, e.g. checking for a preceding < context, or limiting the attribute name to known event handlers rather than the open-ended \bon\w+.

comments.author: breidert
comments.body:
> _Migrated from comment #2 posted 15 Mar 2026 at 12:36 UTC_

Implementation Sketch — MVP Implementation

Issue type: Feature

Component: ai_content_review (new contrib module)

Summary

This issue tracks the MVP implementation of the AI Content Review System — a new contrib module that provides a structured, extensible foundation for AI-assisted content quality reviews in Drupal.

The MVP scope is deliberately narrow: data model, plugin architecture, Drupal AI integration, and a manual trigger. No dashboards, no bulk review, no workflow gates, no automated suggestion application. The goal is a solid, extensible base that future features can build on.

MVP Scope

  • Entity types: Node only
  • AI integration: Drupal AI module (provider-agnostic, via Agents)
  • Suggestions: Text replacements only, displayed for manual copy-paste by the editor
  • Trigger: Manual (user selects one or more criteria and triggers a run)

Data Model

Review Rule (Config Entity)

A named configuration container that defines which criteria apply to one or more entity type/bundle combinations. Multiple Review Rules can apply to the same bundle — the Review tab shows the union of all criteria from all matching rules. Review Rules also serve as the hook for future automated triggers (cron, workflow gates, bulk review).

Key fields:

<pre>
id (machine name)
label
entity_type (e.g. node)
bundles[] (e.g. article, landing_page)
criteria[] (list of Agent IDs)
</pre>

Review Criterion (Plugin, default: Agent-based)

Contains the review logic for a single quality dimension. Receives an entity reference (type:bundle:id), invokes a Drupal AI Agent with structured instructions to load the entity and conduct the review, and returns a structured JSON result (score, severity, explanation, suggestions). All entity and bundle awareness lives in the Agent configuration and instructions.

Review Record (Content Entity, one per criterion execution)

Created each time a criterion is executed. Fully self-contained — each record carries all context needed to understand when and why the review was triggered.

Key fields:

<pre>
id (UUID)
criterion_id
criterion_label
entity_type / entity_id / entity_revision_id
triggered_by (manual | workflow_event | scheduled | bulk)
triggered_by_uid
ai_provider / ai_model
created (timestamp)
status (pending | running | complete | skipped | failed)
score (0–100 | null)
severity (info | warning | critical | null)
explanation (plain language)
error_message (populated on failed status)
suggestions[] → Suggestion
reviewed_at (timestamp)
</pre>

Suggestion (Plugin, default: text replacement)

Nested inside a Review Record. Displayed to the editor for manual application — no automated application or state tracking in MVP.

Key fields:

<pre>
id (UUID)
type (text_replace | ...)
target (field name)
original_value
suggested_value
rationale (1–2 sentences)
</pre>

Pluggable by design — the default covers text replacement; further types (add link, add element, add attribute) can be added via plugins later. Suggestion state tracking (pending | accepted | rejected | applied) is intentionally deferred to a future phase.

Criterion Availability

Which criteria appear on the Review tab of a given entity is determined entirely by configuration. The system loads all Review Rules whose entity_type and bundles[] match the current entity, then displays the union of their criteria[] on the Review tab.

Example: two rules both apply to node:article — one with Brand Voice and Legal Compliance criteria, another with SEO and Reading Level. The Review tab on an article node shows all four criteria.

Creating a Review Rule

Prerequisites: The site builder has created one or more Agents in the Drupal AI Agent UI, each configured with the appropriate instructions for a specific review criterion (e.g. "Review this node for brand voice compliance and return a structured JSON result with score, severity, explanation, and suggestions").

Flow:

  1. Site builder navigates to Administration → AI → Content Review → Rules
  2. Clicks "Add Review Rule"
  3. Fills in:
    • Label (e.g. "Editorial Quality — Article")
    • Entity type (e.g. Node)
    • Bundles (e.g. Article, Landing Page)
  4. Adds one or more criteria via "Add Criterion":
    • Selects an Agent from a list showing each agent's label and description
    • Repeats for each criterion
  5. Saves the rule

The Review tab on all matching entity/bundle combinations immediately reflects the new criteria.

Permissions

Three independent permissions should be defined, assignable to any role:

  • Configure reviews — create and edit Review Rules
  • Run reviews — trigger criterion execution from the Review tab
  • View review records — access Review Records and their results

Agent Response Contract

Every Agent configured as a Review Criterion must return a JSON response conforming to the following structure:

<pre>
{
  "status": "complete | skipped | failed",
  "score": 0–100 | null,
  "severity": "info | warning | critical | null",
  "explanation": "Plain language result or reason for skipping/failing",
  "suggestions": []
}
</pre>

Status meanings:

  • complete — the Agent completed the review normally. Score, severity, explanation, and suggestions are populated.
  • skipped — the Agent ran successfully but could not conduct the review due to missing or insufficient content (e.g. no body text available for a Brand Voice review). Score is null, suggestions are empty, explanation describes why the review was skipped. This is a valid outcome, not an error.
  • failed — the Agent ran but returned a response the module could not parse or that did not conform to this contract. The Review Record is created with status = failed and the raw response stored in error_message for debugging. The editor is shown a clear failure message on the Review tab.

The module must handle all three cases explicitly. A failed record must never silently crash or be discarded — it should always be stored and surfaced to the editor.

Review Tab UI

The Review tab on a node edit page shows all criteria from all Review Rules that match the current entity type and bundle. Criteria are pre-checked by default; the editor can deselect any before running.

Before any review has been run:

<pre>
[x] Brand Voice & Tone
[x] Legal Compliance
[x] SEO Optimization
[x] Reading Level & Clarity

[ Run Selected ]
</pre>

After a run, results appear inline beneath each criterion — score, severity badge, plain-language explanation, and suggestions to copy-paste. Skipped criteria show their explanation in place of a score. Failed criteria show a clear error message with guidance to check the agent configuration. Each criterion shows a "Re-run" button so the editor can re-run it individually after making changes. The tab always shows the latest Review Record per criterion for the current revision.

Execution Flow

Manual run (MVP)

  1. User opens the Review tab on a node — criteria are loaded from all Review Rules matching the current entity type and bundle
  2. User selects one or more criteria and clicks "Run Review"
  3. Each selected criterion executes → the Agent loads the entity and conducts the review → one ReviewRecord per criterion is created
  4. The module validates the Agent response against the expected JSON contract:
    • Valid complete response → Review Record stored with full results and suggestions
    • Valid skipped response → Review Record stored with status = skipped and explanation
    • Invalid or unparseable response → Review Record stored with status = failed and raw response in error_message
  5. Results are displayed inline on the Review tab
  6. Editor manually copies suggested values and applies them to the node
  7. Editor can re-run any individual criterion → a new ReviewRecord is created for the new revision
  8. The tab always shows the latest ReviewRecord per criterion for the current revision

Automated run (future)

  1. An automated trigger (cron, workflow event) loads all Review Rules matching the target entity type and bundle
  2. Each criterion in the matched rules executes → one ReviewRecord per criterion is created with triggered_by = workflow_event | scheduled | bulk
  3. Execution continues identically from step 4 above

Extensibility Requirements

The following extension points must be in place from day one, even if only one implementation ships in the MVP:

  • Review Criterion plugin type — so new criteria can be added without touching core module code
  • Suggestion plugin type — so new suggestion types can be added independently

Out of Scope for MVP

  • Suggestion state tracking and application workflow (accept / reject / apply)
  • Pre-publish workflow gate (Content Moderation integration)
  • Async / queue-based background review
  • Scheduled bulk review
  • Site-wide quality dashboard
  • Content list indicators
  • Notification system
  • Non-node entity types

comments.body:
> _Migrated from comment #4 posted 15 Mar 2026 at 12:56 UTC_ ``` Related issues: + #3573899: [PLAN] Define "Execution Principal" contract for sessionless, queued, and outside-in AI runs ```

The implementation sketch above already captures who triggered a review via `triggered_by` and `triggered_by_uid` on the Review Record, covering provenance and audit requirements on the content review side. The review logic itself lives entirely in Agents. As Agents become aware of their execution principal (per #3573899), that awareness flows into content reviews automatically with no changes needed in the review module.

The same applies to the context control center. Since review criteria are implemented as Agents, any context made available through that system is inherited by reviews for free.

The review module intentionally delegates both identity and context concerns to the Agent infrastructure rather than solving them independently.

comments.author: scottfalconer
comments.body:
> _Migrated from comment #5 posted 15 Mar 2026 at 17:37 UTC_

This looks like the right direction.

I do think two architectural points should be made explicit before we treat the concept as settled:

- Provenance should be explicitly compatible with #3573899: [PLAN] Define "Execution Principal" contract for sessionless, queued, and outside-in AI runs.
triggered_by and triggered_by_uid are useful, but they are not the full execution model that issue is defining. For future background / workflow / outside-in execution, the review model should be able to carry run metadata such as run_id, executor_uid, initiator_uid or initiator_subject, modality, source, and ideally caller_run_id. I’d treat #3573899 as an architectural dependency on the review data model now, rather than assuming identity and context will flow in from Agents “for free.”

- Review Criterion should be the real typed contract boundary, with Agent-based execution as the default implementation.
The summary already says a Review Criterion plugin type must exist from day one, which is good. But the architecture still centers criteria[] as Agent IDs. I think the cleaner split is: Review Rules reference Review Criterion plugins, and one default criterion implementation delegates to an Agent.

That also seems better for Agent Experience as a typed criterion surface is more discoverable to both humans and orchestration agents than opaque Agent selection.

I also think the earlier matrix from #3574179: [Meta] Create AI Review Functionality is still the right organizing device for keeping this extensible: review target, entry point, execution mode/modality, enforcement point, and persisted artifact. That gives us a clean way to keep manual review, future background runs, and eventual workflow integration aligned under one artifact model.

Keeping moderation gates out of the MVP is the right call. When that comes later, Drupal should enforce transitions based on persisted review records, not on a fresh model call during the workflow transition.

A few additional Drupal-shaped details seem worth capturing early, even if not all are MVP:

- treat completed Review Records as append-only evidence, not mutable working state
- make sure the record can distinguish what was reviewed, for example revision / rendered / field-level target, and langcode where relevant
- allow findings that are not tied to a single field, because some review results are page-level or cross-field
- keep access to review records aligned with both review permissions and access to the underlying entity/revision
- consider storing a rule/input hash so review reuse and invalidation are auditable

That would keep the MVP narrow without letting the long-term architecture get fuzzy.

Thank you for the thorough review and for thinking along on the architecture.

Based on your feedback I have updated the implementation sketch with the following changes:

The default plugin implementation delegates to an Agent, but other implementations — wrapping a workflow, custom logic, etc. — are first-class from day one. The Agent is an implementation detail of the default criterion plugin, not the contract itself.

Execution Principal forward compatibility: triggered_by and triggered_by_uid are explicitly noted as MVP-level placeholders. The Review Record data model is intended to be extended with the full Execution Principal envelope (run_id, executor_uid, initiator_uid / initiator_subject, caller_run_id) once #3573899 is implemented. That issue is treated as a future architectural dependency, not an MVP blocker. A modality field (interactive | background | outside_in) has been added now so the record is already aligned with that model from day one.

Data model notes added: The following architectural constraints are now explicitly captured, even where implementation is deferred:

  • Review Records are append-only
  • langcode field added for multilingual support
  • Page-level and cross-field findings supported (target = null)
  • Access to Review Records requires both review permissions and access to the underlying entity/revision
  • A rule/input hash should be stored for audit and future cache reuse

Updated Implementation Sketch

Issue type: Feature

Component: ai_content_review (new contrib module)

Summary

This issue tracks the MVP implementation of the AI Content Review System — a new contrib module that provides a structured, extensible foundation for AI-assisted content quality reviews in Drupal.

The MVP scope is deliberately narrow: data model, plugin architecture, Drupal AI integration, and a manual trigger. No dashboards, no bulk review, no workflow gates, no automated suggestion application. The goal is a solid, extensible base that future features can build on.

Multiple Review Rules can apply to the same bundle — the Review tab shows the union of all criteria from all matching rules. Key fields:

id (machine name)
label
entity_type (e.g. node)
bundles[] (e.g. article, landing_page)
criteria[] (list of criterion plugin instances, each with plugin_id + plugin_configuration)

Review Criterion (Plugin, default: Agent-based)

A plugin type that defines the contract for executing a single quality dimension review. Review Rules reference criterion plugin instances — each instance carries a plugin ID and its configuration. The default plugin implementation delegates to a Drupal AI Agent, passing it structured instructions to load the entity and conduct the review, and parsing the returned JSON result. Other implementations — wrapping an AI workflow, calling custom logic, etc. — are first-class from day one. The Agent is an implementation detail of the default criterion plugin, not the contract itself.

Created each time a criterion is executed. Fully self-contained — each record carries all context needed to understand when and why the review was triggered. Review Records are append-only — no edit operation is defined or permitted. This is enforced both by the absence of an edit permission and programmatically via entity access.

Key fields:

id (UUID)
criterion_id
criterion_label
entity_type / entity_id / entity_revision_id
langcode
triggered_by (manual | workflow_event | scheduled | bulk)
triggered_by_uid (MVP placeholder — see Execution Principal note below)
modality (interactive | background | outside_in)
input_hash (hash of criterion configuration + entity revision at time of review)
ai_provider / ai_model
created (timestamp)
status (pending | running | complete | skipped | failed)
score (0–100 | null)
severity (info | warning | critical | null)
explanation (plain language)
error_message (populated on failed status)
suggestions[] → Suggestion
reviewed_at (timestamp)

Suggestion (Plugin, default: text replacement)

Nested inside a Review Record. Displayed to the editor for manual application — no automated application or state tracking in MVP.

Key fields:

id (UUID)
type (text_replace | ...)
target (field name | null for page-level or cross-field findings)
original_value
suggested_value
rationale (1–2 sentences)

Pluggable by design — the default covers text replacement; further types (add link, add element, add attribute) can be added via plugins later. Suggestion state tracking (pending | accepted | rejected | applied) is intentionally deferred to a future phase.

The following architectural constraints apply from day one, even where full implementation is deferred:

  • Append-only records: Review Records are evidence, not mutable working state. No edit permission is defined. Update operations are denied programmatically via entity access (hook_entity_access()). New reviews always produce new records.
  • Langcode: The langcode field ensures multilingual sites can track which translation of an entity was reviewed.
  • Page-level and cross-field findings: Suggestions where target = null represent findings that are not tied to a specific field — for example, a structural or cross-field observation. These are valid and must not be blocked by the data model.
  • Two-level access: Access to a Review Record requires both the view review records permission and access to the underlying entity/revision. If a user loses access to the entity, they lose access to its Review Records.
  • Rule/input hash: The input_hash field stores a hash of the criterion configuration and entity revision at time of review, enabling future cache reuse and providing an auditable record of what inputs produced a given result.
  • Execution Principal forward compatibility: triggered_by and triggered_by_uid are MVP-level placeholders. The Review Record data model is intended to be extended with the full Execution Principal envelope (run_id, executor_uid, initiator_uid / initiator_subject, caller_run_id) once #3573899 is implemented. The modality field is included now to align the record with that model from day one.

    • Label (e.g. "Editorial Quality — Article")
    • Entity type (e.g. Node)
    • Bundles (e.g. Article, Landing Page)
  4. Adds one or more criteria via "Add Criterion":
    • Selects a criterion plugin type (default: Agent-based)
    • For the default plugin: selects an Agent from a list showing each agent's label and description
    • Repeats for each criterion

The Review tab on all matching entity/bundle combinations immediately reflects the new criteria.

Permissions

Three independent permissions should be defined, assignable to any role:

  • Configure reviews — create and edit Review Rules
  • Run reviews — trigger criterion execution from the Review tab
  • View review records — access Review Records and their results (subject to access on the underlying entity/revision)

Agent Response Contract

Every Agent configured as a Review Criterion must return a JSON response conforming to the following structure:

{
  "status": "complete | skipped | failed",

  "severity": "info | warning | critical | null",

  • complete — the Agent completed the review normally. Score, severity, explanation, and suggestions are populated.
  • skipped — the Agent ran successfully but could not conduct the review due to missing or insufficient content (e.g. no body text available for a Brand Voice review). Score is null, suggestions are empty, explanation describes why the review was skipped. This is a valid outcome, not an error.

Review Tab UI

The Review tab on a node edit page shows all criteria from all Review Rules that match the current entity type and bundle. Criteria are pre-checked by default; the editor can deselect any before running.

> _Migrated from comment #8 posted 16 Mar 2026 at 15:10 UTC_

Me and Artem had an initial call and discussed this - the main question we discuss is where we store the review entity, and if there is a broader use for it (AI recommendation, AI assesment, AI perspective).

For the first we discussed having it in AI or AI Agents module, but reading this it makes sense that its stored in its own module.

For the latter it might just be semantics. But at the same time, if we make the review entity fieldable and bundleable we would make it extendable.

Some other comments on the latest integration suggestion:

  • Should "Agent Response Contract" be "Review Criterion Contract"? I would not use structured output for this, but rather have it use tools to save each part and then the Criterion Plugin can return the response as the executor wants.
  • We should call the permission "View all review records" from the get go. There is a possibility we want a finer granularity here in the future and that name would open up for it and wouldn't need update hooks to update.
  • I think the data requirements for a review seem to follow a matrix rather then a simple selection: scope (Field(s) vs. Full Entity) and state (Markdown vs. Text vs. Rendered). For instance, a SEO analysis of one field usually requires the rendered output of a specific field, while an general content audit might require the full entity context delivered as MD or plain text. (scope and state was suggested by AI, I'm not sure if that's the correct labels). We should store this in the record as two different data points.

Question:

  • Is criterion a naming convention for the plugin only/developers or will it be used in UI. I would opt only for the earlier, because someone will use criteria in singular somewhere by mistake and cause confusion in the UI otherwise.
  • What will be the unique last review that we will show as per the Figma examples - the combination of criterion_id + entity_type + entity_id + entity_revision_id +langcode?

I think in general the issue is setup for Artem to start working on, on Friday.

comments.author: aidanfoster
comments.body:
## Input scope vs output targeting

Building on Marcus's scope and state matrix, some criteria need full page context as input but field-level output targeting.

- SEO is the clearest case. Evaluating it requires the rendered page plus meta tags, because the review depends on how everything works together
- But the actionable feedback (change meta description, shorten H1, etc.) has to land on specific fields for the editor to do anything with it
- Marcus framed scope as field OR full entity. I think some criteria need field AND full entity: full entity as input context, field as output target

Two questions:

- How does the criterion (and the Review Record) capture that combination in the data model?
- How does the agent reliably map findings back to the specific fields that produced them?

comments.author: aidanfoster
comments.body: ## Recommendation: advisory-only MVP, defer actioning to a later phase Christoph proposes a copy-and-paste workflow in which editors manually apply suggestions. The current Figma goes further with per-recommendation Apply buttons. My recommendation is to pull both out of the MVP and ship feedback display only. _The actioning layer needs more design iterations before it's ready._ ### Overview Imagine you're in school and you write an essay to be graded. Then nine different people grade your paper, but you can only read each reviewer's feedback one at a time, and none of the feedback is written directly on the paper. It would require a lot of cognitive load to figure out what feedback you actually want to use. That's close to what the current Review tab asks editors to do. It's more intuitive to think "this hero section has Brand & Legal feedback and reconcile multiple types of feedback on 1 single source content "chunk". Think [Editoria11y module](https://www.drupal.org/project/editoria11y). Lots of different Accessibility violations are possible; the feedback is "fix 3 things on this section." (Which could come from different evaluations) ### Why actioning needs more design work before it ships - **Conflicts between criteria are likely.** SEO and brand voice recommendations will often contradict each other. If I apply both, does last-click win? That's a confusing model for editors. - **Single changes can cascade across the page.** In the Legal Compliance example, changing "T&Cs" to "Terms and Conditions" could touch many places. I'd want to see every affected location before accepting. If the goal is a copy and paste to fix what if that is in 20 places on the page? ### MVP proposal - Show feedback inline as the current design does: score, severity, plain-language explanation, recommendations with reasoning - Treat applying suggestions as a separate design problem with its own UX round before shipping - Let's first tackle making sure the feedback is high-quality. We'll discover how granular it is. Finally, we can think about a UI to help people apply what they want (and very likely they won't want to apply everything). We need to maintain human-in-the-loop control. This keeps the module useful on day one (editors can see issues flagged in plain language) while giving us room to design the actioning experience properly rather than shipping a pattern we'd have to walk back on.

comments.author: dan2k3k4
comments.body:
> _Migrated from comment #3 posted 03 Mar 2026 at 10:23 UTC_

So I believe it will work better if each hosting provider uses their own repository to keep their own hosting-specific changes but depends on the main "demo repo" to get all changes for the "demo project".

Reasons against using different branches per hosting company:
- could lead to accidental merges / branch deletion, although this can be mitigated with branch protection
- branch protection does not specify which usernames have access to that single branch, so any hosting-related dev could potentially write commits to another hosting companies branch, which could lead to other issues if automatic deployments occur from that
- If branch protection does allow such advanced features, then specific usernames would need to be managed per branch, this can add additional complexity if the usernames change often (i.e. one hosting company has a higher rate of employee churn)

I believe the best solution would be to have each hosting company owning their own repo, with only the necessary files to host a project (such as a `.lagoon.yml` file for amazee.io, or the `pantheon.yml` file for Pantheon), and then pull in the "demo repo" project code, such as with Git Submodules.

Repo names should follow a pattern such as: `ai_demos_infra_` like `ai_demos_infra_amazeeio` and `ai_demos_infra_pantheon` - so that we can eventually discover all repos matching the prefix pattern (`ai_demos_infra_`).

DDEV / local development for the project can be in the main repo.

Summary

This meta issue is for the implementation an AI-powered content review system that automatically checks content against defined standards before and after publication.

Reviews should produce a scores for each criterion along with an explanations of the score and concrete suggestions for improvement. In the future it should be possible to automatically apply suggested changes, but this is out of scope for the first iteration.

Sprint Planning Outcome

During sprint planning, the team agreed to scope the first iteration to a functional concept and UX design only. This establishes a shared understanding of the desired functionality before any implementation begins. Based on these outputs, a technical concept will be created that defines the implementation approach and can be executed in the following sprint.

Use Cases (First Iteration)

The initial scope focuses on the following review criteria:

  • Brand compliance: Verify content aligns with brand voice, tone, and style guidelines
  • Brand name and term spelling: Catch incorrect capitalization or formatting of proper names and special terms (e.g. "Drupal Association" not "DrupalAssociation", "React Native" not "ReactNative")
  • Legal compliance: Flag prohibited claims or missing required disclosures
  • Reading level: Assess content for appropriate reading comprehension level and clarity

Implementation

Work is organized into four phases:

  1. Architecture: Define the review pipeline, LLM integration points, criteria format (Markdown templates), and severity classification (blocking / warning / suggestion)
  2. Implementation: Build the "Review with AI" workflow action, analysis logic, and results report UI
  3. Packaging: Release as installable Drupal module(s) and a Drupal recipe; include demo content with intentional issues and automated test scripts
  4. Drupal CMS integration: Integrate into Drupal CMS

Related

This feature should integrate with the Context Control Center for managing brand guidelines, legal requirements, and editorial standards. Integration timing depends on when a first version of the Context Control Center becomes available. If no version is available at the time of implementation, the first iteration can source context (e.g. brand guidelines) from elsewhere (e.g. configuration).

comments.author: aidanfoster
comments.body:
> _Migrated from comment #7 posted 24 Feb 2026 at 03:30 UTC_

Prior Architecture Ideas Related to AI Review

This work has been explored in depth over the past several months. Two existing module candidates should be evaluated as starting points, and there is an architecture document covering the longer-term "apply feedback" workflow.

Existing Module Candidates

  • AI Content Advisor (Dima / itech4web) — Analyzes rendered entities (view modes) to provide scoring and feedback. Distinct because it evaluates the final HTML output, not just raw field data. No security coverage yet, no CCC integration, cannot apply suggestions back to fields. Dima and Aidan collaborated on the broader AI Content Studio architecture (see below).
  • Adriano Dias' hackathon prototype ("AI QA Gates") — Plugin-based review system built at the Brussels EU Commission AI hackathon. Features configurable review criteria, content moderation/workflow gate integration (block state transitions based on review severity), and a working per-suggestion approve/reject UI. Already being refactored to use AI Context (CCC) and AI Agents instead of hardcoded prompts. A video walkthrough of Adriano's prototype is available in the Slack channel.

Reviewing Data on Fields vs. Rendered Nodes

  • Review should happen against the rendered page, not individual fields. Cross-field concerns — acronym first-use rules, reading flow, accessibility, SEO heading structure — require full page context to evaluate correctly.
  • Applying feedback makes the most sense at the field level, so changes can be targeted and saved as non-destructive draft revisions.
  • Canvas note: Canvas UI does not persist data in its unpublished revisions, so rendered-content review would only work against published nodes in the Canvas context. This scopes the first iteration firmly to the traditional node edit form (Fields, Paragraphs, Layout Builder).

Potential UI Considerations

  • Adriano's prototype has a working UI with per-suggestion approve/reject controls and severity-based content moderation gates — worth reviewing as a UX starting point (video in Slack channel)
  • Scoring display options to explore: traffic-light indicators, circle/radial charts, numeric percentage scores — ideally the editor configures which display style per review criterion
  • Where does the "Review with AI" trigger live? Tab on node edit form, sidebar panel, or both?
  • How do blocking vs. non-blocking results surface differently? (e.g., blocking issues prevent workflow transitions, warnings are informational)

Architecture

  • Needs a simple, consistent schema for review criteria — each criterion should include Do / Do Not examples paired together, plus scored examples: Score 0 = "This content contains multiple brand name misspellings and prohibited claims", Score 0.5 = "Tone is mostly on-brand but includes informal language in the intro", Score 1 = "Fully compliant with brand voice, no issues detected"
  • The LLM returns a 0–1 score per criterion; the UI maps that to whatever display widget the editor configured (traffic light, percentage, etc.)
  • Adriano's prototype had a really interesting feedback loop concept: when the AI produces poor feedback or improper suggestions, those get piped back into the rules as exclusion examples, improving accuracy over time. This "learning from bad suggestions" pattern should be considered in the architecture
  • Non-determinism is a real challenge — the same content reviewed twice may produce different scores. Consider caching review results and only re-running on content change or rule change

Integration with CCC

  • CCC integration can happen in a future iteration — the first version can source review criteria from module configuration, with a clear migration path to CCC
  • When CCC integration lands, this would introduce a new Context Control Center Use Case = Reviews, so that any CCC context item could be scoped to review tasks
  • This makes the review framework generic — the same scoring mechanism (traffic light / circle chart / numeric score) could be used for anything: brand compliance, SEO, AEO, accessibility, legal compliance, reading level, or fully custom criteria defined in CCC
  • CCC sub-contexts could enable scenario-specific review rules (e.g., stricter legal review for financial services pages vs. blog posts)
  • Dynamic CCC context (e.g., live analytics data) could eventually feed into review scoring — "this page has high bounce rate" as an input to content quality assessment

Future Considerations — Reviewing Recommendations, Then Applying Them (AI Content Studio)

Aidan & Dima mapped out an "AI Content Studio" architecture that extends review into a full feedback-and-apply loop. This was deferred in favour of focusing on CCC stability first, but the functional concept and UX work should align with this direction. Key elements:

  • The "evaluate the whole, update the parts" render-to-field mapping is the core unsolved technical challenge — tracing rendered output back to the source field/entity/delta so that approved suggestions can be applied programmatically
  • Editorially module (drupal.org/project/editorially) is a great existing UX reference for inline content feedback patterns, as it displays feedback directly on the rendered node
  • Whole-page evaluation followed by itemized suggestions with per-suggestion approve/reject
  • One-click application of approved changes to the correct fields as draft revisions
  • Traffic-light / percentage-based scoring dashboard on node edit forms with user-defined criteria (brand compliance, SEO, tone & voice, custom rules via CCC)
  • Background agent evaluation — re-score content on save, on schedule, or when CCC rules change
  • Site-wide overview dashboard for filtering and sorting content by score and compliance status

Related Modules in This Space

Role Status
Rendered entity analysis & scoring Dev (no security coverage)
Field-level suggestions sidebar (AI Core submodule) Has a stable (no security coverage)
Plugin framework for attaching AI actions to form fields Stable (AI Core 1.2.0)

comments.author: scottfalconer
comments.body:
> _Migrated from comment #8 posted 07 Mar 2026 at 01:25 UTC_

My recommendation is that the ticket should frame AI Review around the evidence each criterion needs and the risk of the action it is informing, rather than around a persona or UI.

Suggested product statement:

AI Review should support the minimum review target required by each criterion and action risk, expose that capability through multiple entry points and execution modes, and always produce one consistent persisted review result.

To keep the concept/UX work clear, I'd separate these dimensions:

  • review target: field/component, revision, rendered output
  • entry point: inline check, sidebar/panel, workflow gate, background trigger
  • execution mode / modality: interactive, background, outside-in (and source if needed)
  • enforcement point: advisory, warning, transition gate, publish gate
  • persisted output: one canonical review artifact regardless of how review was launched
  • Whole-page/rendered review is necessary for some criteria, but it should not become the sole product model.

In addition, I'd the concept include a matrix with:

  • criterion
  • criteria source/version
  • minimum review target
  • entry point
  • execution mode
  • enforcement point
  • persisted output

Problem/Motivation

There has been recent confusion about the development sprint process due to a number of issues such as:

  • Contributor planning tab being locked when not all partners have filled it in
  • Not knowing when an issue should be tagged for innovation vs product development
  • Issues being tagged for a sprint without consultation with the module maintainer

And, although the general process has been explained during dev planning meetings, we will have new partners join regularly, and we need to point them to the docs so they can understand the process.

Lastly, this will be a place where we can refine our processes, so leadership and management can better work together.

Proposed resolution

Some of this documentation may already exist. It needs to be pulled together and structured and gaps filled.

Create document that includes:

  • Adding availability to the planning sheet
  • Attending and participation in meetings
  • How to demo work
  • etc

We can assume the partner has gone through the contract and general onboarding process and contributors have already filled out the contributor profile form. Meaning, the partner has been handed off from general onboarding and is starting to engage.

Target date or deadline

February 2026

Remaining tasks

  • Write the docs
  • Figure out where to put it
  • Put it there and start using it

AI usage (if applicable)

[ ] AI Assisted Issue
This issue was generated with AI assistance, but was reviewed and refined by the creator.

[ ] AI Assisted Code
This code was mainly generated by a human, with AI autocompleting or parts AI generated, but under full human supervision.

[ ] AI Generated Code
This code was mainly generated by an AI with human guidance, and reviewed, tested, and refined by a human.

[ ] Vibe Coded
This code was generated by an AI and has only been functionally tested.

merge_requests.title: Issue #3586406: Add gitlab-cli-setup skill + README workflow notes
merge_requests.author: jjchinquist
merge_requests.description:
## Summary

Adds `skills/gitlab-cli-setup/` — a discoverable, agent-agnostic guide for contributors using AI coding assistants (Claude Code, ChatGPT/Codex, Cursor, etc., or just `bash`) to drive `git.drupalcode.org` from the command line.

Closes #3586406.

## Structure

- **`SKILL.md`** — entry point: `glab` install, env-var auth, PAT scope, the **`@file` gotcha** (already destroyed two issue descriptions on this initiative — flagged loudly), MCP alternative note, file index, out-of-scope.
- **`issues.md`** — issue CRUD via `glab`; drupal.org companion via `api-d7` (read-only); drupal.org issue lifecycle and credit attribution as web/commit-message flows since drupal.org has no public REST write API.
- **`merge-requests.md`** — both drupalcode.org MR flavors (coordination repo vs core/contrib issue-fork flow), state and metadata updates, comments.
- **`code-review.md`** — diff fetching, `glab mr checkout` (handles issue-fork remote setup automatically), inline review comments anchored via `position`+`diff_refs`, resolving threads.
- **`pipelines.md`** — pipeline status on a branch/MR, retrying jobs.

## README

- New **Skills** section pointing at the new `SKILL.md`.
- New workflow note: **Sprints are GitLab Milestones (not Iterations)** — documents the convention so contributors stop hunting for an Iteration picker.

## Acceptance criteria

- [x] AC-1: discoverable `SKILL.md` exists for the Marketing AI Initiative
- [x] AC-2: documents `glab` install + env-var auth pattern, PAT creation + `api` scope recommendation
- [x] AC-3: flags the `glab api -f field=@file` data-loss gotcha with the `DESC=$(cat file)` workaround; cites note 807944
- [x] AC-4: covers ~5 day-to-day patterns (per topic file) — list/view/create/comment/update on issues; create/view/state on MRs; diff/checkout/inline-comment/resolve on code review; status/trace/retry on pipelines; plus the unauthenticated `discussions.json` read fallback
- [x] AC-5: cross-links rather than duplicates drupal.org GitLab docs
- [x] AC-6: explicit out-of-scope (just patch-vs-MR for first pass)

## Verified empirically before shipping

- `glab` CLI flags (`--draft`/`--ready`/`--label`/`--description`, etc.) — caught one fabricated flag (`--description-file`) and replaced it before commit.
- `/changes` API endpoint shape on `project/canvas` MR `!1`.
- `source_project_id` ≠ parent `project_id` for issue-fork MRs.
- drupal.org `api-d7` issue status enum confirmed for values 1–8 and 13–18 (gaps at 9–12).

## Notes

- Agent-agnostic phrasing throughout — no Claude/ChatGPT/Cursor-specific tool names. Snippets are pure shell so any agent or human can run them.
- First-draft scope — content will iterate after review per the work item's own framing.

---

*Drafted with the assistance of AI (Claude Code / Opus 4.7).*

issues.title: Url alias not translated
issues.author: drupalbot
issues.description:
>>> [!note] Migrated issue Reported by: [jonas139](https://www.drupal.org/user/2873401) Related to !13 >>>

Problem/Motivation

When using the AI translate module to translate entities that have a manual url alias set, the alias is not translated unless you enable 'Generate automatic url alias' in the source entity.

I'm having this behaviour with AI core version 1.2.8, Drupal core 11.2.8 and version 1.3 of this module.

Steps to reproduce

1. Setup AI and AI translate module
2. Enable 'Url alias' in the 'Entity reference translation' setting of the configuration page of the AI translate module
3. Go to the translate tab of an exisiting entity that has a manual url alias set (not generated automatically) and click 'translate with X' (where X is the used AI model).
4. After the translation is done, look at the url alias of the translated entity. This is the same as the one from the source entity

Proposed resolution

Always translate the url alias when 'url alias' is checked in the entity reference translation setting.

Remaining tasks

User interface changes

API changes

Data model changes

comments.author: svendecabooter
comments.body:
> _Migrated from comment #4 posted 03 Mar 2026 at 08:17 UTC_

A path_alias can have a language assigned to it, but is not marked as translatable:
The path_alias entity does not have the "translatable" flag set in Drupal core, so as far as I can see that entity type does not allow content translation workflows, so that means this wouldn't work by design in the current implementation.

It might also be hard to have automatic AI translations for path aliases anyway... If they are set to /some-readable-words that could be translated I guess, but if the alias is an abbreviation or something, like /srw, the AI will probably not know what to do with it?

[AI TMGMT](https://www.drupal.org/project/ai_tmgmt) uses the TMGMT "processors" ... and in this case TMGMT module define and use a path processor for the `path` field

of course as @svendecabooter mentioned ... it's not always "desired".

And as generic issue ... it seems `TextExtractor` too restrictive with the data that should be extracted/translated - it doesn't allow alterations/customisations ...

Problem/Motivation

The ai_provider_configuration form element #3556181: Create a form element for selecting providers and the ai.provider_config schema type #3552774: Create linkable schema for providers were designed to work together. In #3552774: Create linkable schema for providers comment #15, @marcus_johansson described the intended workflow:

  1. Set up the `ai_provider_configuration` form element in your form.
  2. Store the value using the `ai.provider_config` config schema.
  3. Load the configured provider using a helper method from the plugin manager.

However, step 2 doesn't work seamlessly because the form element and the schema use different value formats:

Form element returns:

<?php
[
  'provider' => 'openai',
  'model' => 'gpt-4.1',
  'config' => ['temperature' => 0.7],
]
?>

Schema expects:

use_default: false
provider_id: openai
model_id: gpt-4.1
configuration:
  temperature: 0.7

This means:

  • #config_target cannot be used with the ai_provider_configuration element, because ConfigFormBase::storeConfigKeyValueMap() would write the element's value (with provider/model/config keys) directly to config, which doesn't match the ai.provider_config schema (which expects use_default/provider_id/model_id/configuration keys). The element also sets '#default_value' => NULL in its getInfo(), which prevents ConfigFormBase::loadDefaultValuesFromConfig() from auto-populating the value.
  • Every consumer module must manually translate between the two formats in submitForm() and when setting #default_value, adding error-prone boilerplate.

The documentation added in #3556181: Create a form element for selecting providers (in ai_provider_configuration_element.md) even shows this manual translation in its "Saving to Configuration" example, confirming the gap.

Steps to reproduce (required for bugs, but not feature requests)

  1. Create a ConfigFormBase with an ai_provider_configuration element.
  2. Try to use #config_target pointing at a config key with type: ai.provider_config.
  3. Observe that:
    • The default value is not auto-populated from config (because getInfo() sets #default_value => NULL).
    • On submit, the wrong key names are written to config (provider instead of provider_id, etc.).
    • The use_default boolean is never set.

Proposed resolution

Add #config_target support to the ai_provider_configuration element by:

  1. Adding a toConfig() static method that translates the element's provider/model/config value to the schema's use_default/provider_id/model_id/configuration format.
  2. Adding a fromConfig() static method that does the reverse (for populating #default_value from config).
  3. These can be used as the ConfigTarget callable pair, enabling:
'#config_target' => new ConfigTarget(
  'my_module.settings',
  'provider_config',
  toConfig: [AiProviderConfiguration::class, 'toConfig'],
  fromConfig: [AiProviderConfiguration::class, 'fromConfig'],
),

Or another method to achieve the same mapping logic automatically.

Remaining tasks

  • Implement the translation layer or value format change
  • Update the element's getInfo() so #default_value doesn't unconditionally block ConfigFormBase auto-population when #config_target is used
  • Update documentation examples
  • Add test coverage for #config_target usage with the element

> _Migrated from comment #8 posted 23 Mar 2026 at 19:59 UTC_

Another issue was discovered when trying to make #config_target work with this element:

The AiProviderConfiguration::valueCallback() method incorrectly uses full #parents-based paths to index into the $input parameter (e.g., NestedArray::getValue($input, array_merge($element['#parents'], ['provider_model']))), but $input as passed by FormBuilder::handleInputElement() is already scoped to the element's #parents position in the POST data — so attempting to traverse into $input['provider_config']['provider_model'] fails when $input is already ['provider_model' => 'openai__gpt-4', 'config' => [...]]. This causes $selected_value to always be empty during form submission, meaning the callback returns empty provider/model values and the selected configuration is never persisted. The fix is to use relative keys (e.g., $input['provider_model']) instead of full parent-based paths when extracting values from $input, while continuing to use the full $element['#parents']-based paths only when accessing $form_state->getUserInput() (which contains the complete, unscoped input tree).

Added a fix for this too.
