issues.title: RegexpGuardrail::processOutput() unconditionally returns a PassResult without ever running the configured regex pattern against the AI output text
issues.author: drupalbot
issues.description:
>>> [!note] Migrated issue Reported by: [ajv009](https://www.drupal.org/user/3653917) Related to !1338 >>>

[Tracker]
Update Summary: RegexpGuardrail::processOutput() unconditionally returns PassResult, post-generate guardrails never scan AI output
Short Description: RegexpGuardrail does not apply regex checks to AI output, making post-generate guardrails no-ops
Check-in Date: 03/22/2026
Metadata is used by the AI Tracker. Docs and additional fields here.
[/Tracker]

Problem/Motivation

RegexpGuardrail::processOutput() unconditionally returns a PassResult without ever running the configured regex pattern against the AI output text:

public function processOutput(
  OutputInterface $output,
): GuardrailResultInterface {
  // This guardrail only processes input, not output.
  return new PassResult('Output processing is not applicable for this guardrail.', $this);
}

This means any guardrail set that assigns a regexp_guardrail plugin to the post-generate phase will silently pass all AI output without scanning it.

The practical impact is significant: the AI Guardrails PII recipe (#3577498) assigns all four PII guardrails (email, phone, credit card, IBAN) to both pre-generate and post-generate phases and claims bidirectional protection. The pre-generate side works correctly, but the post-generate side is completely non-functional because of this bug.

An LLM could return a response containing email addresses, credit card numbers, or other PII from RAG sources or training data, and the guardrail would not catch it.

RestrictToTopic has the same no-op processOutput() pattern, but fixing that is more complex since it requires an LLM provider call. This issue focuses on RegexpGuardrail where the fix is straightforward.

Steps to reproduce

  1. Install the AI module (1.3.x) and enable it
  2. Create a guardrail of type "Regexp Guardrail" with pattern /[a-z0-9._%+\-]+@[a-z0-9.\-]+\.[a-z]{2,}/i (email detection)
  3. Create a guardrail set and assign this guardrail to both pre-generate and post-generate phases
  4. Assign the guardrail set to a chat interaction (e.g. the chat generation explorer at /admin/config/ai/explorers/chat_generator)
  5. Send a prompt that would cause the LLM to include an email address in its response (e.g. "What is the contact email for the Drupal security team?")
  6. Expected: The response should be blocked by the post-generate guardrail because it contains an email address
  7. Actual: The response passes through unfiltered. The post-generate guardrail never runs the regex check.

AI modules enabled: ai (1.3.x), any AI provider module

AI provider: Any (the bug is in the guardrail plugin, not the provider)

Proposed resolution

Implement actual regex checking in RegexpGuardrail::processOutput(). The ChatOutput::getNormalized() method returns a ChatMessage (or StreamedChatMessageIteratorInterface), which provides getText() to extract the response text.

The implementation should mirror processInput() but operate on the output:

  1. Check if the output is a ChatOutput instance
  2. Get the normalized output (ChatMessage)
  3. Handle the case where the normalized output is a StreamedChatMessageIteratorInterface (streamed responses may need special handling or can pass through with a note)
  4. Extract the text via getText()
  5. Run the configured regex pattern against it
  6. Return StopResult on match, PassResult otherwise

Remaining tasks

  • Implement processOutput() in RegexpGuardrail
  • Decide how to handle streamed responses (StreamedChatMessageIteratorInterface). Options: skip scanning with a log message, or buffer and scan.
  • Add tests covering post-generate guardrail scanning
  • Consider whether RestrictToTopic::processOutput() should also be addressed (separate issue)

Related issues

  • #3577498: [Meta] AI PII Guardrails Recipe - the recipe that exposed this bug
  • #3579088: Guardrail plugins only evaluate the last message - related guardrail processing issue
  • #3577790: Add validation to regex guardrail configuration - related regexp guardrail improvement
  • #3578846: AiGuardrailSet does not declare config dependencies - related guardrail infrastructure fix

Thanks for the review @marcus_johansson, good catch on the tool calls.

Pushed a follow-up that extends processOutput() to also scan the tool call payload after the assistant text check. It uses the existing ChatMessage::getRenderedTools() output, runs json_encode() on it, and applies the same regex against the resulting string. On a match it returns the same StopResult as the text path. processInput() is untouched.

I went with the json_encode approach instead of a custom recursive walker over ToolsFunctionOutput::getArguments(). The walker version was about 50 lines with two new private helpers, and most of it was just plumbing for the nested array case. The encoded form covers strings nested anywhere in the tool call (flat args, arrays, future shapes) in three lines. The trade-off is that JSON keys (function name, argument names) are also in the haystack, but for PII and similar patterns that is the safer failure mode for a guardrail anyway.

Added two unit tests: a tool argument that matches an email pattern is blocked, a clean argument passes. Validated the blocking test fails against the pre-MR processOutput().

One thing I want to flag in case I read your comment differently than you meant it: I interpreted "tool output" as the LLM-authored tool calls on the assistant side (the model trying to send PII to a tool). If you also meant scanning the incoming tool result messages on the input side (role tool, fed back into the next turn), that path is part of the broader "processInput only checks the last message" gap that #3579088: Guardrail plugins only evaluate the last message already tracks, so I left it alone here to keep this MR scoped.

Transparency note: This follow-up was drafted with highly supervised usage of Claude Code. I verified the diff and tests personally. If anything in my process needs adjusting, please flag it and I'll course correct.

comments.author: AJV009
comments.body:
> _Migrated from comment #13 posted 08 Apr 2026 at 18:24 UTC_ ``` - Assigned: ajv009 + Assigned: Unassigned - Status: Needs work + Status: Needs review ```

Thanks for the follow-up, @marcus_johansson. Pushed a new commit that replaces the json_encode() approach entirely with a direct walk over the tool call arguments.

On comment 1 (the NIT about escape artifacts): processOutput() now iterates $normalized->getTools(), then $tool->getArguments(), and feeds each $argument->getValue() through a small private collectScalarStrings() helper that recurses into arrays and Traversable, stringifies scalars, and casts objects with __toString. Each string becomes its own entry in the $haystacks list, so the regex runs against the raw characters (newlines, tabs, quotes, backslashes) with no JSON escape step anywhere in the scan path.

On comment 2 (the try/catch + JSON_THROW_ON_ERROR + empty fallback): this one turned out to be moot after the rewrite, because there is no json_encode() call left to wrap. Wanted to call that out explicitly rather than silently skip the comment. Happy to walk back to a json_encode + exception-safe variant if you'd rather keep the encoded scan as a second layer, but I landed on "no encoding at all" because it was the cleanest way to honour the first comment.

And my bad, I realised the previous diff was actually double-wrapping the arguments: getRenderedTools() already calls Json::encode() on the args internally, so running another json_encode() over the result was encoding on top of encoding. Walking getArguments() directly sidesteps that entirely.

Added three unit tests for the new behaviour: testProcessOutputBlocksMatchInNestedToolArgument covers a deeply nested array argument (exercises the recursion), testProcessOutputScansRawControlCharactersInToolArguments runs a /\t/ pattern against a real tab inside a tool argument (this one would have silently passed under the old json_encode path, so it's the direct regression guard for your NIT), and testProcessOutputDoesNotMatchJsonEscapeForms is the inverse, asserting that a pattern looking for the literal two-character escape form \t does NOT fire against the same raw tab. processInput() is untouched and the "guardrails only evaluate the last message" thread stays in #3579088: Guardrail plugins only evaluate the last message.

Transparency note: This follow-up was drafted with highly supervised usage of Claude Code. I verified the diff, ran the stash-and-rerun validation, and reviewed the output personally. If anything in the approach needs adjusting, flag it and I'll course correct.

Short Description: [One-line issue summary for stakeholders]
Check-in Date: MM/DD/YYYY
Metadata is used by the AI Tracker. Docs and additional fields here.
[/Tracker]

Problem/Motivation

When creating a new guardrail using either the RestrictToTopic or RegexpGuardrail plugins, the following PHP warnings appear in the logs:

Warning: Undefined array key "invalid_topics_present_message" in Drupal\ai\Plugin\AiGuardrail\RestrictToTopic->buildConfigurationForm() (line 141)
Warning: Undefined array key "valid_topics_missing_message" in Drupal\ai\Plugin\AiGuardrail\RestrictToTopic->buildConfigurationForm() (line 155)
Warning: Undefined array key "violation_message" in Drupal\ai\Plugin\AiGuardrail\RegexpGuardrail->buildConfigurationForm() (line 128)

The buildConfigurationForm() methods in both plugins use the Elvis operator (?:) to access configuration keys that do not yet exist when a guardrail is first created. In PHP 8, this triggers an "Undefined array key" warning because ?: evaluates the array access before applying the fallback — unlike ??, which checks key existence first and suppresses the warning.

Steps to reproduce

  1. Enable the AI module with guardrails support
  2. Navigate to create a new Regexp or RestrictToTopic guardrail
  3. Observe PHP warnings in the logs

Proposed resolution

Replace ?: with ?? for the three affected keys so undefined array keys are handled safely:

<?php
// Before (triggers PHP warning when key is absent):
$this->configuration['violation_message'] ?: 'default message';

// After (null-safe, no warning):
$this->configuration['violation_message'] ?? 'default message';

The same change applies to 'invalid_topics_present_message' and 'valid_topics_missing_message' in RestrictToTopic.php.

Remaining tasks

  • Fix RegexpGuardrail::buildConfigurationForm() — violation_message key
  • Fix RestrictToTopic::buildConfigurationForm() — invalid_topics_present_message and valid_topics_missing_message keys

User interface changes

None. The form renders and saves correctly — this is a warning cleanup only.

issues.title: Automator FWA buttons give no user feedback when values don't visibly change
issues.author: drupalbot
issues.description:
>>> [!note] Migrated issue Reported by: [petar_basic](https://www.drupal.org/user/3626336) >>>

[Tracker]

Short Description: [One-line issue summary for stakeholders]

Metadata is used by the AI Tracker. Docs and additional fields here.
[/Tracker]

Problem/Motivation

When an editor clicks a Field Widget Action (FWA) button backed by an ai_automator, the AJAX call runs, the throbber appears and disappears, but there is no user feedback confirming that the automator actually ran.

This is especially visible on the ClassificationOptionsSelect widget for taxonomy reference fields:

- User clicks Suggest Topic on a field that already has a value selected.
- AI runs and returns the same value that is already selected.
- Form state does not change — dropdown stays on the same option.
- Editor sees nothing happen and assumes the button is broken.

Steps to reproduce (required for bugs, but not feature requests)

Please provide information like AI modules enabled, which AI provider, browser, etc.
1. Install ai_automators and field_widget_actions.
2. Configure an automator with worker_type: field_widget_actions on a taxonomy reference field (e.g. field_topic with a Topics vocabulary).
3. Set up ClassificationOptionsSelect FWA on the form display.
4. Open a node edit form where field_topic already has a value selected.
5. Click the Suggest Topic button.
6. Observe: AJAX runs, throbber shows+hides, nothing else changes on screen.

Same effect when the AI returns the same value already entered on any other automator FWA plugin.

Proposed resolution

In AutomatorBaseAction::populateAutomatorValues(), after saveFormValues(), add a status message with the values the automator generated.

$aiConfig->set('base_field', ($formState->getValue('automator_mode') === 'base') ? ($formState->getValue('automator_base_field') ?? '') : '');

issues.title: RestrictToTopic guardrail: add semantic topic matching mode
issues.author: drupalbot
issues.description:
>>> [!note] Migrated issue Reported by: [marcus_johansson](https://www.drupal.org/user/385947) >>>

[Tracker]
Update Summary: [One-line status update for stakeholders]
Short Description: Add a semantic matching mode to the RestrictToTopic guardrail so LLM-identified topics are matched against the configured list by meaning rather than exact string equality.
Check-in Date: MM/DD/YYYY
[/Tracker]

Problem/Motivation

The restrict_to_topic guardrail in src/Plugin/AiGuardrail/RestrictToTopic.php asks an LLM which of the configured topics are present in the input text, then uses an exact-string in_array() check to place each returned topic into the valid or invalid bucket:

foreach ($topics_present as $topic) {
  if (\in_array($topic, $valid_topics)) { ... }
  elseif (\in_array($topic, $invalid_topics)) { ... }
}

The LLM does not reliably return exactly the strings from the configured list. It may generalise ("banana" → "banana fruit"), pluralise, translate, add qualifiers, or return a semantically equivalent synonym. When that happens the returned topic matches neither bucket, the guardrail silently drops it, and the user request either passes through unchecked or the valid_topics_missing branch fires incorrectly.

The fixed prompt also instructs the model to "return a valid json list of which topics are present" but does not constrain it to return verbatim strings from the input list, so the drift is expected behaviour rather than a model defect.

Proposed resolution

  • Check if this makes sense?
  • Add a matching_mode setting to the guardrail configuration form with at least two options: exact (current behaviour, default for backward compatibility) and semantic (new).
  • In exact mode, keep the existing in_array() logic unchanged.
  • In semantic mode, change the LLM prompt to force the model to answer using only the exact strings from the configured list (e.g. "For each topic present in the text, return the closest matching topic from the provided list verbatim, or omit it if nothing matches"), so the returned values can still be bucketed with in_array().
  • Alternatively, or as a third mode, run a second LLM pass (or an embedding-based similarity check) that maps each returned free-form topic back to the nearest configured topic above a similarity threshold, and bucket on that.
  • Consider adding a configurable similarity threshold for the embedding variant, and surface unmatched topics in the result metadata for debugging.
  • Decide whether semantic mode should require a separate embeddings provider configuration or reuse the existing llm_provider / llm_model chat provider.
  • Add kernel test coverage for both modes, including the "banana" vs "banana fruit" style drift case.

Update Summary: [One-line status update for stakeholders]
Short Description: [One-line issue summary for stakeholders]
Check-in Date: MM/DD/YYYY
Metadata is used by the AI Tracker. Docs and additional fields here.
[/Tracker]

Problem/Motivation

The AI module currently has no operation type for extractive question answering — a task where the model receives a question and a context passage and extracts the answer span directly from the context, along with a confidence score and the character positions where it found the answer.

This is a well-established NLP task supported by Hugging Face's question-answering pipeline with popular models like deepset/roberta-base-squad2 and distilbert/distilbert-base-cased-distilled-squad (both have active inference endpoints). Unlike chat-based QA, extractive QA is deterministic, fast, and grounded — the answer comes directly from the source text with no hallucination risk.

There is currently no way for Drupal modules to call extractive QA models through the AI module's normalized provider interface.

Proposed resolution

Add a new extractive_question_answering operation type following the same pattern as text_classification and image_classification.

Example usage

use Drupal\ai\OperationType\ExtractiveQuestionAnswering\ExtractiveQuestionAnsweringInput;

$input = new ExtractiveQuestionAnsweringInput(
  'What is the capital of France?',
  'The capital of France is Paris, which is also the largest city in the country.'
);

$result = \Drupal::service('ai.provider')
  ->createInstance('huggingface')
  ->extractiveQuestionAnswering($input, 'deepset/roberta-base-squad2', ['my_module']);

$items = $result->getNormalized();
foreach ($items as $item) {
  $answer = $item->getAnswer();    // "Paris"
  $score  = $item->getScore();     // 0.98
  $start  = $item->getStart();     // 25
  $end    = $item->getEnd();       // 30
}

Remaining tasks

issues.title: Unable to override the 'Calling agents'  / 'Contacting agents' text
issues.author: m4olivei
issues.description:
### Problem/Motivation

The deepchat conversational UI shows the the text 'Calling agents..' or 'Contacting agents..' when the system is off delgating to agents. 

Depending on the client / use case, this language can be confusing and/or unfamiliar, ultimately not helpful to the end user.

This task is to allow this text to be overriden in configuration. I'd also suggest that the text be made consistent. By my read 'Contacting agents..' is more appropriate, as its more general than 'Calling', which we probably mean in the developer sense of 'Calling an API', but may users would read as a literal phone call.

#### Steps to reproduce

* Configure ai_chatbot module with ai_agents module. 
* Issue a user query that delegates to agents.

**Expected result**

Site administrators can customize the 'Calling agents..' / 'Contacting agents..' text to read whatever is appropriate for their site, eg. 'Analyzing your data..'

**Actual result**

Hardcoded text reading 'Calling agents..' or 'Contacting agents..' is used without any way to override it.

### Proposed resolution

Add a new block configuration setting, "Agent Delegation Message", to the DeepChatFormBlock alongside the existing "Loading Message" setting in the "Messages" section.

### Remaining tasks

* Consensus on using 'Contacting agents..' consistently rather than having both 'Contacting agents..' and 'Calling agents..'
* Consensus on the setting name, eg. "Agent Delegation Message"
* Implement additional configuration
* Default to 'Contacting agents..'

### User interface changes

Additional setting for "Agent Delegation Message" to override the default 'Contacting agents..' message.

issues.title: Dispatch AiExceptionEvent and allow subscribers to replace the response (graceful failover)
issues.author: drupalbot
issues.description:
>>> [!note] Migrated issue Reported by: [marcus_johansson](https://www.drupal.org/user/385947) Related to !1514 >>>

[Tracker]
Update Summary: [One-line status update for stakeholders]
Short Description: Dispatch a new AiExceptionEvent from ProviderProxy::wrapperCall() so subscribers can rewrite the exception message or substitute an OutputInterface, enabling third-party failover without patching core.
Check-in Date: MM/DD/YYYY
[/Tracker]

Problem/Motivation

Projects are shipping the AI module's features and third parties want to customise how failures surface. Two real needs today:

  • Customise the error message. When OpenAI reports a budget overrun, the raw exception surfaces as Drupal\ai\Exception\AiRequestErrorException: Error invoking model response: Budget has been exceeded! Current cost: 1.133637, Max budget: 1.0 thrown from ProviderProxy::wrapperCall(). The floating-point precision and the raw "Budget has been exceeded" text leak implementation detail to the end user. Downstream modules currently have no extension point to rewrite this before it propagates.
  • React to a failure without taking the caller down with it. When the primary provider exceeds quota or hits a rate limit, site owners want to transparently fall back to a secondary provider (or a cached response, or a canned apology) without the caller having to catch and retry. Right now ProviderProxy::wrapperCall() always rethrows and there is no way for a subscriber to substitute a valid OutputInterface.

Both #3542496: Dispatch AiExceptionEvent when a provider throws an exception (dispatch an exception event) and a graceful-failover mechanism are being built or discussed in parallel. This issue does both in one patch because they share the dispatch site and the same event object.

Proposed resolution

Three coordinated parts, all landing together because they touch the same three lines of ProviderProxy::wrapperCall() and the same event class.

Part A — Dispatch AiExceptionEvent (from #3542496: Dispatch AiExceptionEvent when a provider throws an exception). Add a new event class that carries the original exception and an overridable message, preserving the exception class when a subscriber rewrites the text so existing catch (AiQuotaException $e) blocks keep working:

namespace Drupal\ai\Event;

use Symfony\Contracts\EventDispatcher\Event;

final class AiExceptionEvent extends Event {

  private string $message;

  public function __construct(
    public readonly \Exception $exception,
  ) {
    $this->message = $exception->getMessage();
  }

  public function setMessage(string $message): void {
    $this->message = $message;
  }

  /**
   * Returns the exception that should be thrown by the proxy.
   *
   * The original exception type is preserved so callers keep their
   * specific catch blocks (AiQuotaException, AiRateLimitException, ...).
   * Only the message is swapped if a subscriber rewrote it.
   */
  public function getException(): \Exception {
    if ($this->message === $this->exception->getMessage()) {
      return $this->exception;
    }
    $class = get_class($this->exception);
    return new $class($this->message, $this->exception->getCode(), $this->exception);
  }
}
Dispatch it in ProviderProxy::wrapperCall(), inside the existing try/catch block, for every caught exception type. Dispatch by class (no event-name string), consistent with the MR !846 review discussion:


catch (\Throwable $e) {
  $event = new AiExceptionEvent($e);
  $this->eventDispatcher->dispatch($event);
  // Handled-response path - see Part B.
  if ($forced = $event->getForcedOutputObject()) {
    return $forced;
  }
  throw $event->getException();
}
Move the existing per-type $this->loggerFactory->get('ai')->error(...) calls out of ProviderProxy and into an event subscriber on AiExceptionEvent so logging behaviour is extensible too.


Part B — Allow subscribers to replace the response (failover). Add a forced-output slot on the event, mirroring PreGenerateResponseEvent:


// On AiExceptionEvent.
use Drupal\ai\OperationType\OutputInterface;

protected ?OutputInterface $forcedOutputObject = NULL;

public function getForcedOutputObject(): ?OutputInterface {
  return $this->forcedOutputObject;
}

public function setForcedOutputObject(OutputInterface $output): void {
  $this->forcedOutputObject = $output;
}
If a subscriber called setForcedOutputObject(), the proxy returns that output instead of throwing. If no subscriber sets one, behaviour is identical to Part A alone: rethrow with the (possibly rewritten) message. This is the minimum surface that lets a third-party module implement graceful failover - for example, a module that sees an AiQuotaException on openai can call a backup provider via AiProviderPluginManager, build an OutputInterface from the response, and set it on the event; the original caller receives a successful response and never sees an exception.


Part C — Preserve the request context on the event. For failover to be useful, subscribers need to know which provider/model/input failed. Extend the constructor:


public function __construct(
  public readonly \Exception $exception,
  public readonly ?string $requestThreadId = NULL,
  public readonly ?string $providerId = NULL,
  public readonly ?string $operationType = NULL,
  public readonly ?string $modelId = NULL,
  public readonly mixed $input = NULL,
  public readonly array $configuration = [],
  public readonly array $tags = [],
) { ... }
ProviderProxy::wrapperCall() already has all of these in scope at the point of dispatch (it constructs PreGenerateResponseEvent from them a few lines earlier). All new arguments are nullable / defaulted so existing instantiations, including test fixtures, keep working.


Example: a failover subscriber in a third-party module. With Parts A-C in place, a module can implement provider failover without patching AI core:


namespace Drupal\my_failover\EventSubscriber;

use Drupal\ai\AiProviderPluginManager;
use Drupal\ai\Event\AiExceptionEvent;
use Drupal\ai\Exception\AiQuotaException;
use Drupal\ai\Exception\AiRateLimitException;
use Drupal\ai\OperationType\Chat\ChatInput;
use Symfony\Component\EventDispatcher\EventSubscriberInterface;

final class FailoverSubscriber implements EventSubscriberInterface {

  public function __construct(
    private readonly AiProviderPluginManager $aiProvider,
  ) {}

  public static function getSubscribedEvents(): array {
    return [AiExceptionEvent::class => 'onException'];
  }

  public function onException(AiExceptionEvent $event): void {
    // Only failover on quota/rate-limit, and only for chat.
    if (!($event->exception instanceof AiQuotaException
        || $event->exception instanceof AiRateLimitException)) {
      return;
    }
    if ($event->operationType !== 'chat' || !$event->input instanceof ChatInput) {
      return;
    }
    // Avoid looping if the backup itself threw.
    if ($event->providerId === 'anthropic') {
      return;
    }
    $backup = $this->aiProvider->createInstance('anthropic');
    $output = $backup->chat($event->input, 'claude-3-5-sonnet-latest', $event->tags);
    $event->setForcedOutputObject($output);
  }
}
The caller of the original chat() call receives the backup provider's response and is unaware anything failed.


Backwards compatibility. Part A only changes the thrown exception when a subscriber rewrites the message; even then, the class is identical, so existing catch (AiQuotaException $e) keeps working. With no subscriber, behaviour is byte-for-byte identical to current code. Part B adds one optional behaviour (forced output) which is a no-op unless opted into. Part C adds constructor arguments that are all optional, so new AiExceptionEvent($e) remains a valid call. No existing callers of ProviderProxy need to change.


Why one issue instead of two. Parts A and B touch the same three lines of ProviderProxy::wrapperCall() and the same event class. Splitting them means landing an event that has a known missing capability (no way to recover gracefully), then landing a follow-up that rewrites what just landed. Landing together also means the tests cover the interaction (subscriber rewrites the message and sets a forced output - the output wins, the message is irrelevant), which isn't exercised if the two land separately.


Two MRs: 1.x preserves the original exception class, 2.0.x wraps in AiResponseErrorException. The rethrow behaviour is deliberately different on the two branches:


    

  • 1.x MR - keep the exact current behaviour: the thrown exception is the same class as the original (AiQuotaException, AiRateLimitException, etc.), with only the message swapped when a subscriber rewrites it. This preserves byte-for-byte compatibility for every site currently using catch (AiQuotaException $e) and friends in 1.x.
    • 

  • 2.0.x MR - normalise the rethrow into a single AiResponseErrorException that wraps the original exception as its $previous. Callers that care about the specific cause use $e->getPrevious() to reach the original AiQuotaException / AiRateLimitException / etc. This lets callers write one catch (AiResponseErrorException $e) for all provider-side failures while still being able to branch on the underlying class when needed. It also means the rewritten-message path no longer has to reconstruct the original exception class by reflection - the wrapper is always the same type, and the original is available untouched via getPrevious(). This is a breaking change, which is why it ships in 2.0.x only.
    • 



Both MRs share the event class, forced-output slot, and context properties. The only divergence is in ProviderProxy::wrapperCall()'s rethrow statement and in AiExceptionEvent::getException(). Tests split accordingly: the 1.x MR asserts the caller sees the original class; the 2.0.x MR asserts the caller sees AiResponseErrorException with getPrevious() returning the original.


Remaining tasks:


    

  • Implement AiExceptionEvent with message, forced-output, and context properties (Parts A + B + C).
    • 

  • Update ProviderProxy::wrapperCall() to dispatch, honour forced output, and rethrow with the event's exception.
    • 

  • Move per-type logging from ProviderProxy into Drupal\ai\EventSubscriber\AiExceptionLoggingSubscriber and register it in ai.services.yml.
    • 

  • Unit test: subscriber that rewrites the message - assert the caller sees the rewritten text and the original exception class.

comments.body:
> _Migrated from comment #7 posted 22 Apr 2026 at 09:35 UTC_ ``` - Assigned: abhisekmazumdar + Assigned: Unassigned ```

I have addressed several issues found during review and pushed two commits to the MR.Which still needs review.

1. Restored ClientExceptionInterface wrapping (backwards compatibility)

The unified catch block was silently dropping the AiBadRequestException wrapping that the old code applied to ClientExceptionInterface. Callers such as ai_chatbot's DeepChatApi check instanceof AiBadRequestException to identify this case. The wrapping is now restored before the exception is passed into AiExceptionEvent.

2. Fixed instanceof checks in AiExceptionEventSubscriber

switch (get_class($exception)) cannot match interface names, it returns the concrete class, so the ClientExceptionInterface::class case never fired. Replaced the switch with an if/elseif/else instanceof chain.

3. Added code comment explaining the intentional early return

The forced-output early return in ProviderProxy bypasses post-processing events by design, consistent with the same pattern used for PreGenerateResponseEvent. A subscriber that provides a forced output is responsible for the safety of that output. A comment documents this to prevent future confusion.

4. Return types and final on AiExceptionEventSubscriber

Added : void on onAiException() and : array on getSubscribedEvents(). These are required by Drupal coding standards and keep the class consistent with AiExceptionEvent, which is already fully typed.

Marked the class final because this is a logging subscriber with no intended extension points. Third-party modules should respond to AiExceptionEvent by registering their own subscribers, not by extending this one. Making it final makes that boundary explicit.

5. New unit tests for AiExceptionEventSubscriber

The tests covering every instanceof branch, the default fallback, and getSubscribedEvents() return shape.

AI assisted (Claude Code): reviewed the code, plan out fixes, wrote unit tests and draft this comment. All commits, decisions are mine and thoroughly reviewed.

Problem/Motivation

Currently, the AI Automators module allows for automated content generation and field processing, but it does not natively integrate with the AI module's Guardrails system.

Site builders using Automators (e.g., for automatic translation, summarization, or SEO generation) should be able to apply Guardrail Sets to these processes. issues.title: Add streaming-aware guardrails that can buffer and evaluate content in real-time using start/stop regex patterns
issues.author: drupalbot
issues.description:
>>> [!note] Migrated issue Reported by: [marcus_johansson](https://www.drupal.org/user/385947) Related to !1489 >>>

[Tracker]
Update Summary: [One-line status update for stakeholders]
Short Description: Add streaming-aware guardrails that can buffer and evaluate content in real-time using start/stop regex patterns
Check-in Date: MM/DD/YYYY
[/Tracker]

Problem/Motivation

Follow up from #3518963: [Meta] Create the concept of Guardrail agents.

Currently, guardrails in the AI module only operate on complete output. When a streaming response is used, a guardrail either has to implement NonStreamableGuardrailInterface (which forces the entire stream to be reconstructed via reconstructChatOutput() before the guardrail can process it) or it receives the full ChatOutput after the stream has finished. In both cases, the user has already received all the streamed content before any post-generation guardrail can act on it - or the streaming experience is completely broken by waiting for the full response.

This means there is no way to evaluate content during streaming. For example, if an LLM starts outputting harmful content, the user sees the entire response before a guardrail can intervene. There is also no way to buffer suspicious portions of a stream and decide whether to release or suppress them in real-time.

What is needed is a new guardrail capability that can hook into the streaming iteration itself. The guardrail itself should be able to dynamically decide when to start buffering and when to stop - using configurable start and stop regex patterns. When a start regex matches incoming streamed content, the system begins buffering instead of yielding to the consumer. Content accumulates while streaming continues, and when the stop regex matches (or the stream ends), the guardrail evaluates the buffered content and decides whether to release, suppress, or rewrite it.

This approach is intentionally dynamic - rather than having a single hardcoded buffer strategy in the iterator, each streaming guardrail controls its own buffering behavior. This allows different guardrails to watch for different patterns and buffer different portions of the stream independently. The current hardcoded buffer logic in StreamedChatMessageIterator should eventually be replaced by this guardrail-driven approach, so that all streaming content evaluation goes through the guardrail system.

Proposed resolution

  • Introduce a new interface (e.g. StreamableGuardrailInterface) for guardrails that can operate on streaming content, with methods for providing a start regex, a stop regex, and a processing callback for buffered content.
  • Extend the StreamedChatMessageIterator (or create a guardrail-aware decorator/wrapper) so that during iteration, it can check each chunk against registered streaming guardrails' start patterns.
  • When a start regex matches, the iterator begins buffering content instead of yielding it to the consumer. Streaming continues internally, but the buffered content is held back.
  • When the stop regex matches (or the stream ends), the buffered content is passed to the guardrail for evaluation. The guardrail returns a result - pass (release the buffer), stop (suppress and replace with a guardrail message), or rewrite (modify the buffered content before releasing).
  • Content that does not match any start regex should continue to stream through normally with no delay.
  • The existing hardcoded buffer logic in StreamedChatMessageIterator (e.g. shouldFlush()) should be migrated to use this new guardrail-driven buffering system, so that all streaming content evaluation is handled dynamically through guardrails rather than baked into the iterator. This work can however only be done when guardrails can be placed everywhere.
  • Consider how multiple streaming guardrails interact if more than one has overlapping start/stop patterns.
  • Determine how the GuardrailsEventSubscriber should register streaming guardrails with the iterator - possibly via the PostGenerateResponseEvent or by wrapping the iterator before it is returned to the consumer.

comments.author: Marcus_Johansson
comments.body:
> _Migrated from comment #2 posted 30 Mar 2026 at 08:04 UTC_

How other frameworks handle streaming guardrails

For reference, here is how some other AI frameworks approach this (from Claude Code):

  • Guardrails AI - The closest to what is proposed here. Each validator controls its own buffering via an overridable _chunking_function(). Returns nothing while accumulating, returns a result when enough context exists. Sentence-based splitting by default, but each validator can define custom chunking.
  • NVIDIA NeMo Guardrails - Uses a global chunk_size with a sliding window for overlap. All rails share the same chunk boundaries. Has a "stream first, moderate in parallel" mode, but warns that objectionable content may already have been sent.
  • Microsoft Semantic Kernel - Filters can detect streaming and wrap the async stream to intercept/transform chunks. No built-in buffering abstraction - each filter implements its own.
  • Spring AI - Similar wrap-the-stream approach via StreamAdvisor using reactive operators for custom accumulation.
  • Amazon Bedrock - Binary sync/async toggle. Sync buffers before sending, async streams immediately with background moderation.
  • LangChain, LlamaIndex, OpenAI Agents SDK - No mid-stream guardrail support. Guardrails run post-completion only.

The per-guardrail buffering approach (as in Guardrails AI) is the most flexible and aligns with the dynamic design proposed in this issue. See https://guardrailsai.com/guardrails/docs/how-to-guides/custom_validators#streaming

> _Migrated from comment #7 posted 14 Apr 2026 at 15:15 UTC_ ``` - Assigned: abhisekmazumdar + Assigned: Unassigned - Status: Active + Status: Needs review ```

An initial implementation has been pushed to the issue branch.

What was implemented

  • New StreamableGuardrailInterface extending AiGuardrailInterface with three methods: getStartRegex(), getStopRegex(), and processStreamedBuffer(string $bufferedContent): GuardrailResultInterface.
  • StreamedChatMessageIterator extended with per-guardrail state tracking. Each flushed chunk is passed through processStreamingGuardrails(), which checks start/stop patterns and suppresses output while buffering is active.
  • End-of-stream flush: if a guardrail is still buffering when the stream ends, the buffer is evaluated and the result is applied.
  • addStreamingGuardrail() and getStreamingGuardrails() added to StreamedChatMessageIteratorInterface.
  • GuardrailsEventSubscriber::applyPostGenerateGuardrails() updated to detect StreamableGuardrailInterface instances and register them with the iterator directly, bypassing the regular post-generate path.
  • Unit tests covering: pass-through with no guardrails, PassResult releasing the buffer, StopResult suppressing and replacing, RewriteOutputResult rewriting, end-of-stream flush, empty start regex, and content before the trigger streaming normally.

Cross-check against proposed resolution

  1. New interface with start regex, stop regex, and processing callback: ✅
  2. Iterator checks each chunk against start patterns: ✅ Runs after the existing URL-safety buffer flushes, so the two layers are independent.
  3. Start regex triggers buffering, output suppressed: ✅
  4. Stop regex or end-of-stream triggers evaluation, pass/stop/rewrite handled: ✅ All three result types are handled.
  5. Non-matching content streams normally with no delay: ✅ Early return when no guardrails are registered; no change to hot path.
  6. Migrate hardcoded shouldFlush() logic to guardrail-driven buffering: Intentionally skipped per the issue note ("can only be done when guardrails can be placed everywhere"). The existing URL-safety buffer is untouched.
  7. How multiple streaming guardrails interact with overlapping patterns: See open question below.
  8. GuardrailsEventSubscriber registers streaming guardrails with the iterator: ✅ via PostGenerateResponseEvent before the regular post-generate loop runs.

Open question: multiple guardrails with overlapping patterns

The current implementation chains guardrails: each guardrail sees the output of the one before it. If guardrail A suppresses a chunk, guardrail B receives an empty string rather than the original text.

The proposed resolution describes guardrails as operating "independently", buffering "different portions of the stream independently". Achieving true independence would require each guardrail to track the original text, not the already-processed text.

Is the chaining behaviour acceptable for this issue, or should each guardrail operate on the original chunk independently?

AI disclosure: Brainstormed the Code, test, and comment with AI assistance and manually reviewed, tested before posting.

This needs more work - the buffer for testing the guardrail and the buffer that gets outputted should be different.

So, it would be ok that it outputs part of something that gets blocked.

Right now the streaming is too chompy, it outputs one sentence at a a time which is a clear feature regression. And that is even without a guardrail attached. As long as the regex and block is checked before the last chunk it would be fine.

Think of this:

This will not render:
<a href="https://www.my-malicious.com/website"

This will render but be blocked:
<a href="https://www.my-malicious.com/website">

This means that you can see parts of secret names, but even then I would argue that our solution is better then anything that exists currently.

The other option is that we have late streaming buffers - meaning we keep a sentence in buffer and then start yielding one word at a time form that sentence, while buffering the next.

Setting this back to work, and I think we have to aim for 1.5.x for this, since its not in as much of a hurry.

> _Migrated from comment #16 posted 21 Apr 2026 at 14:03 UTC_ ``` - Assigned: abhisekmazumdar + Assigned: Unassigned - Status: Needs work + Status: Needs review ```

I feel I have addressed both issues raised in #3582179: Add streaming-aware guardrails that can buffer and evaluate content in real-time using start/stop regex patterns.

Issue 1: Detection buffer and output buffer decoupled.

The lookahead window was suppressing $text immediately on every chunk, even before a start pattern was found. The window is now detection-only: text flows to the consumer unchanged until a match is confirmed. Only the triggering chunk is suppressed.

Issue 2: Sentence-at-a-time output fixed via word-level splitting.

The URL-safety buffer in StreamedChatMessageIterator already flushes in sentence-sized chunks (on \n or after 100 characters). To give consumers smooth token-level output without changing the URL-safety or guardrail detection logic, getIterator() now splits each flushed chunk word-by-word before yielding.

private function splitIntoWordTokens(string $text): array {
  preg_match_all('/\S+\s*|\s+/', $text, $matches);
  return $matches[0];
}

Each flushed sentence becomes a sequence of individual word tokens yielded one at a time. The $this->messages array and reconstructChatOutput() are unaffected, they still operate on sentence-level data. Guardrail detection, URL-safety checking, and pattern matching all continue to operate on the full sentence buffer as before.

Regarding pre-match leakage: agreed this is the correct tradeoff. An incomplete tag like <a href="https://www.my-malicious.com/website" cannot render, so yielding it before the pattern closes is safe. The renderable form <a href="https://www.my-malicious.com/website"> is what gets caught, and by that point the regex fires.

Manually verified via the Chat Generation Explorer with streaming enabled: output now appears word-by-word with no sentence-level batching, and guardrail blocking continues to work correctly.

MR updated.

issues.title: Integrate Symfony AI's Platform component as a replacement for AI Providers
issues.author: drupalbot
issues.description:
>>> [!note] Migrated issue Reported by: [mxr576](https://www.drupal.org/user/315522) Related to !1214 !1259 !1250 !1337 >>>

[Tracker]
Update Summary: [One-line status update for stakeholders]
Short Description: [One-line issue summary for stakeholders]
Check-in Date: MM/DD/YYYY
Metadata is used by the AI Tracker. Docs and additional fields here.
Additional Collaborators: @lussoluca, @fago
[/Tracker]

Problem/Motivation

The AI module currently implements its own AI provider abstraction layer. A successful proof-of-concept (#3565674: Create first version of the abstraction layer using Symfony AI v0.1.0) demonstrated that Symfony AI's Platform component (symfony/ai-platform) is a viable foundation for this functionality. A kick-off call was held between @marcus_johansson, @fago, @lussoluca, @admitriiev, and @mxr576 to align on moving forward with a formal integration effort.

The Symfony AI Platform component provides a unified interface for working with various AI models and providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Azure, Gemini, VertexAI, and more). Adopting it as the underlying abstraction in the AI module would align Drupal's AI ecosystem with a well-maintained, PHP-native standard rather than maintaining a bespoke layer. The long-term goal is for this integration to replace the current AI Provider system.

Steps to reproduce (required for bugs, but not feature requests)

Please provide information like AI modules enabled, which AI provider, browser, etc.

Phase 1: Analyse, PoC

The scope of this issue is to explore and evaluate different integration patterns, reach a community agreement on the best approach by weighing pros and cons, and then commit the accepted solution to the AI module.

Two parallel approaches will be developed and compared:

  1. Greenfield integration (led by @lussoluca and @mxr576): A clean-slate integration that adheres as closely as possible to the Platform component's API, exposing its abstractions directly to Drupal. Backward and forward compatibility with the current AI Provider API is explicitly not a goal, or only a best-effort concern.
  2. Backward-compatibility-focused integration (led by @fago and @admitriiev): An integration that wraps or maps the Symfony AI Platform API onto the existing AI module's provider abstractions, preserving compatibility for existing consumers as much as possible.

Both approaches will be evaluated against each other. The evaluation criteria include design quality, developer experience, maintainability, compatibility implications, and the migration path for existing AI provider implementations and consumers. Once an approach is agreed upon, it will be merged into the AI module as the canonical implementation.

Remaining tasks

  • Implement the greenfield integration prototype (@lussoluca, @mxr576)
  • Implement the backward-compatibility-focused prototype (@fago, @admitriiev)
  • Review and compare both prototypes as a group
  • Document pros and cons for each approach
  • Reach agreement on the preferred approach
  • Commit the accepted solution to the AI module - TBD whether this gets committed to 1.x as well with deprecation triggers

Phase 2: Prototype development

Plan is that 2.x introduces Platform Provider Plugins, Multi-instance, Drops support for AI-Providers

  • We keep BC for operation types / business layer and select a configured platform instance to use per operation type
  • We probably need to keep operation-type focused API and provide a Symfony-based replacement
  • Generally, we mark to-be-removed API as deprecated and establish new Symfony-platform focused API
  • After that, we continue flushing out the new Symfony-focused API.

Additional information

  • POC issue: #3565674: Create first version of the abstraction layer using Symfony AI v0.1.0
  • Symfony AI Platform component documentation: https://symfony.com/doc/current/ai/components/platform.html
  • Symfony AI Platform Component on GitHub: https://github.com/symfony/ai/tree/main/src/platform
  • Note: The Symfony AI components are currently under development but @chertel already confirmed that the Platform's APIs are close to stable so we can start working on the integraiton.

Optional: Other details as applicable (e.g., User interface changes, API changes, Data model changes)

Related Symfony AI issues

Road to Symfony AI v1.0

Issues we opened or keep an eye on:

  • [Platform] Add a human-readable label to models - would reduce the necessary glue code for exposing a Plaform implementation (as a plugin) in Drupal
  • [Platform] Improve documentation of thrown exceptions - would make Platform APIs consumption more resilient at the integration level and exposing it as primary APIs for LLM communication in Drupal AI
  • [Platform] Clarify BC policy for model IDs exposed by model catalogs - Model IDs used to be stored in configuration, we need to know if we could rely on model IDs returned by Platform implementation and what are the rules we must follow
  • 2026.04.11: [Platform] Introduce Provider abstraction and model routing layer - iif this lands, we should revisit our integration bridge, since AI Platform plugins may effectively become Provider plugins, which would introduce a naming overlap on our side. =] 0.8.0 is out with this change, we have to decide how to adopt

comments.body:
> _Migrated from comment #8 posted 21 Feb 2026 at 12:36 UTC_

BC-impact analysis

I'm starting with a BC-impact analysis (with the help of AI). That's how I'm approaching it:

  • Obvious BC-breaking changes are requiring Drupal 11 and API-changes for implementing providers. That's clear and not the focus, leaving that aside.
  • Question is what APIs are our "API-consumers" using and which changes would have the highest impact. This is the STEP 1 of the analysis
  • Further, possible impact is for BC-breaking changes is in configuration for modules and recipes. This would would be mostly be possible change in provider-IDs and provider settings. I'm going to make a separate analysis for that, focusing on the same ai-modules and the recipes cover in the ai-dashboard. So this is STEP2.

STEP 1- BC-analysis for code

As potentially breaking APIs I've identified and grouped our APIs in the following main items:

  • Provider: getDefaultProviderForOperationType()`
  • Provider: Operation Type Interfaces -> e.g. `$provider->textToImage($input, $model)`
  • Provider form helpers — getSimpleProviderModelOptions('chat') + loadProviderFromSimpleOption('openai__gpt-4o')
  • Chat Input/Output - ChatMessage, ChatOutput, ChatInput
  • Multimodal input types — e.g. ImageFile passed via ChatMessage.
  • Streamed-Chat-API: `StreamedChatMessageIteratorInterface`
  • EmbeddingsInput and EmbeddingsOutput — used by $provider->embeddings($input, $model)
  • Chat-Tool-Calling: `ChatInput::setChatTools($toolsInput)`, ToolsInputInterface, Toolsoutput
  • FunctionCall plugin system for defining tools
  • AI request events (Pre/PostGenerateResponse)
  • VDB provider interface (AiVdbProviderInterface)

As most relevant API-consumers I've identified the following, things being an ai-core submodule is marked with *:

  1. ai_automators *
  2. ai_assistant_api *
  3. ai_chatbot *
  4. ai_ckeditor *
  5. ai_content_suggestions *
  6. ai_search *
  7. ai_logging / ai_external_moderation *
  8. ai_agents
  9. canvas_ai
  10. ai_context
  11. field_widget_actions

* = AI core submodule

Evaluation criteria

I'm going to use the following impact rating scale:

Rating Meaning
- Not used by this consumer
None Used, but no impact — fully backward-compatible, no
changes needed
Low Few call sites, easy to adapt
Medium Moderate usage, manageable changes
High Heavy usage, significant rework needed

Evaluation results

(big table displays broken here, attaching html file instead)

comments.author: fago
comments.body:
> _Migrated from comment #9 posted 21 Feb 2026 at 13:35 UTC_ > **File** [30-api-consumer-matrix.md](https://www.drupal.org/files/issues/2026-02-21/30-api-consumer-matrix.md)

Evaluation Summary

Attaching evaluation result matrix as markdown file (html tables are not rendered correctly).

API-Usage Overview:

  • Provider: getDefaultProviderForOperationType() — Used by 6 consumers but fully BC-compatible; no consumer code needs to change if method signature is preserved.
  • Provider: Operation type interfaces ($provider->chat(), $provider->embeddings(), etc.) — Used by 7 consumers, ai_automators being the heaviest with 5 different operation types; preservable with wrapper interfaces.
  • Provider form helpers (getSimpleProviderModelOptions, loadProviderFromSimpleOption) — Used by 5 consumers for building provider/model dropdowns in settings forms; low impact if helper methods are preserved.
  • Chat I/O: ChatInput, ChatMessage, ChatOutput — The most widely used API (9 of 11 consumers), with ai_agents being the heaviest at 30+ call sites; high volume but no impact if types are preserved as wrappers.
  • Multimodal: ImageFile via ChatMessage — Only used by 3 consumers (ai_agents, ai_automators, canvas_ai) for passing images to vision models.
  • Streaming: StreamedChatMessageIteratorInterface — Used by 4 consumers; ai_assistant_api extends and produces the stream, while chatbot, ckeditor, and agents consume or type-check it.
  • Embeddings: EmbeddingsInput, EmbeddingsOutput — Only used by ai_automators and ai_search for vector embedding generation; isolated from the chat API.
  • Chat tool-calling: setChatTools(), ToolsInput, ToolsOutput — Narrow usage in only 2 consumers (ai_assistant_api and ai_agents); the bridge between Drupal's FunctionCall plugins and the LLM's tool-calling protocol.
  • FunctionCall plugin system (#[FunctionCall], FunctionCallBase) — Used by 6 consumers, but ai_agents (33 plugins) and canvas_ai (14 plugins) account for the bulk; no impact if the Drupal plugin system is preserved alongside Symfony AI's #[AsTool].
  • AI events (Pre/PostGenerateResponseEvent) — Only subscribed to by ai_logging and ai_agents_explorer, both AI-core-adjacent; very low external impact.
  • VDB interface (AiVdbProviderInterface) — Exclusively used by ai_search (4 files); completely isolated from the chat/provider migration.

Highest impact

  1. Chat I/O (ChatInput/ChatMessage/ChatOutput) — used by 9 of 11 consumers. ai_agents is the heaviest user (30+ sites). Under BC approach: None to Low impact if types are preserved.
  2. getDefaultProviderForOperationType() — used by 6 consumers. Under BC approach: None if method signature preserved.
  3. Operation type interfaces ($provider->chat(), etc.) — used by 7 consumers. ai_automators uses the widest variety (chat, embeddings, textToImage, textToSpeech, speechToText). Under BC approach: None to Low if interfaces preserved.
  4. FunctionCall plugin system — used by 6 consumers, but ai_agents (33 plugins) and canvas_ai (14 plugins) account for the bulk. Under BC approach: None if #[FunctionCall]/FunctionCallBase preserved.

Step2 : Config BC evaluation

Impact rating:

Rating Meaning
- No config stores provider/model references
None Config exists but uses abstract references (__default__,
default_vision, empty) — no migration needed
Low Few config entries, simple string replacement in update
hook
Medium Multiple entries or complex format, needs careful
migration
High Many config entities per site (scales with
content/usage), complex migration

Evaluation results Matrix

Consumer Stored provider ID refs Combined provider__model refs Provider-specific config blobs Scale (entities per site)
ai.settings (core defaults) * Low (separate provider_id + model_id per op-type, ~10 entries) - - Low (singleton)
ai_automators * None-Low (sentinel tokens default, default_json, default_vision; specific provider only if user overrides) - Medium (automator_configuration_* params in opaque blob) High (one per rule, scales with content types x fields)
ai_assistant_api * Low (llm_provider + llm_model; often __default__) - Medium (llm_configuration opaque blob) Medium (one per assistant, typically 1-5)
ai_ckeditor * - Low (provider field, combined format; usually empty = default) - Low (one per text format x 7 plugins, usually empty)
ai_content_suggestions + FWA * - Low (singleton plugins.id + per-field settings.model; often empty) - Medium (singleton + per-field widget action)
ai_search * - Medium (embeddings_engine + chat_model, critical for search) Low (embeddings_engine_configuration dimensions) Low (typically 1 search server)
ai_agents - - - - (no provider/model stored; resolves from global default at runtime)
canvas_ai - - - - (no provider/model stored; resolves from global default at runtime)
ai_context Low (separate provider_id + model_id in singleton; often empty) - - Low (singleton)
Recipes
drupal_cms_ai None (__default__ token for llm_provider, empty model) - - None
drupal_cms_ai_ckeditor - None (empty provider strings = use default) - None
ai_recipe_image_classification None (default_vision, default_json tokens) - - None
ai_recipe_content_classification None (expected same pattern as image_classification) - - None
ai_recipe_seo_optimizer None (default_json token) - - None
ai_recipe_llm_optimized_content - - - - (no AI config at all)

Evaluation Summary

  • All recipes are safe — Every analyzed recipe uses abstract references (__default__, default_vision, default_json, or empty strings). No recipe hardcodes a specific provider ID. Recipe-installed configs survive migration without changes.
  • ai_agents and canvas_ai store nothing — They always resolve provider/model from global defaults at runtime, so zero config migration needed.
  • Three distinct storage formats exist — (1) Separate provider_id + model_id fields (ai.settings, ai_assistant_api, ai_context), (2) combined provider__model string (ai_ckeditor, ai_content_suggestions, ai_search), (3) opaque plugin_config blobs (ai_automators).
  • Provider-specific config might need attention — ai_assistant_api (llm_configuration) and ai_automators (automator_configuration_*) store opaque provider-specific params (temperature, max_tokens, etc.). These don't change with provider IDs, but may need migration if the underlying provider configuration schema changes in the new implementation.
  • Scale is manageable — Most config is singletons or a handful of entities.
    ai_automators can scale to dozens of rules per site, but most use sentinel tokens (default, default_json) rather than specific provider IDs.

comments.body:
> _Migrated from comment #15 posted 27 Feb 2026 at 11:11 UTC_

I posted my MR - please take a look at the MR description for a summary.

I tried to achieve backwards compatibility while establishing symfony-platform as the new API, deprecating the old provider API:

  • It seems to work out fine for translating existing provider usage calls (e.g. chating with a model), via a converter service which translates our Input/Output classes into messages expected by symfony platform.
  • Providers can be used with existing API in a backwards-compatible manner (deprecated), or via a new symfony-platform API focussed API.
  • What remains problematic is keeping support for defining providers with the old API. Those would not work with the new symfony-platform first API. That would require another translation layer, translating symfony messages back to to our Input/Output classes. Not sure this is feasible. This seems to be feasible also, as proven by my second MR.

I'd see the main following possible scenarios based upon my PoC:
- Go with backwards-compatibility for using providers, but not for defining providers. This is a BC-break, so release this as 2.x. In 3.x we could drop the BC-layer for using providers also. Upgrade for ecosystem should be managable, make sure you upgrade provider modules, then update.
- Go with backwards-compatibility for using providers, two-way. This is still a BC-break since it would require Drupal 11/Symfony 7, so it needs to be released 2.x (not 1.4.x) also. In 3.x we could drop the BC-layers. The upgrade for the ecosystem should be easy thanks to the BC layers.
- Break both APIs for using and defining providers at the same time. Upgrade for ecosytem becomes harder.

I need to look into the other approach and digest next - I did not do it so far to not influence mine.

comments.author: fago
comments.body:
> _Migrated from comment #18 posted 05 Mar 2026 at 13:19 UTC_

I took a look at @mxr576's approach and I really like:

  • introducing config entities for solving the multi-instance issue
  • introducing a new plugin, next to providers - so we have a clean old/vs new api

Given the full-BC experimentation I think it would be viable to create a full-BC layer for that, i.e. ship the new platform API next to the existing provider API + deprecate the existing provider API while keeping it fully intact.

For that we'd need the following bc-layer:
* For working with providers (e.g. load provider by model / operation-type + do things): Translate this to platform API
* For existing provider plugins: Proxy platform API calls to existing provider API methods

But we'd have to define and agree on the new API first. E.g. as proposed in my MR, we need some API providing the discovery by operation type, but also by provider+model:

  • loadPlatformProvider($id, $model_id)
  • loadPlatformProviderByDefinition($definition)
  • loadDefaultPlatformProvider($op_type)

When we support multi-instance, we'd have to clarify which instance loadPlatformProvider($id) would default to also.

comments.author: mxr576
comments.body:
## Proposed architecture plan: Symfony AI 0.8.0+ integration

#### `AiPlatformProvider`: config entity + plugin type

```php
interface AiPlatformProviderInterface extends ProviderInterface, PluginInspectionInterface {}
```

The config entity gives a vendor connection a machine name, makes it storable and exportable via CMI, and allows it to be referenced by multiple `AiPlatform` entities without duplicating credentials. The plugin type owns the instantiation logic for each concrete `ProviderInterface` implementation (one plugin per bridge: OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.). The instantiated plugin IS the working `ProviderInterface`, no wrapper method needed.

Credentials are referenced via the Key module inside the plugin's config blob. The `getName()` implementation returns the config entity's machine name, giving unambiguous identity in logs and error messages even when two entities use the same underlying plugin type. (`ProviderInterface::getName()` — resolved by design: A concern raised during the review of [symfony/ai#1923](https://github.com/symfony/ai/pull/1923#discussion_r3068491595) was that `ProviderFactory::create()` always returns 'openai' as the name regardless of which API key or region is used.)

#### `AiPlatform`: config entity + plugin type

```php
interface AiPlatformInterface extends PlatformInterface, PluginInspectionInterface {}
```

The config entity gives a `PlatformInterface` configuration a machine name, makes it referenceable from other config (agents, chat configs, search indexes, etc.), and stores which `AiPlatform` plugin is used plus that plugin's configuration blob. The plugin type owns the instantiation logic and defines what configuration is needed for each concrete `PlatformInterface` implementation.

Each concrete plugin maps to one `PlatformInterface` implementation:

- A plugin wrapping `\Symfony\AI\Platform\Platform` exposes config fields for which `AiPlatformProvider` config entities to reference and which `AiPlatformModelRouter` plugin to use.
- A plugin wrapping `FailoverPlatform` references a primary `AiPlatform` config entity and failover config.
- A plugin wrapping `TraceablePlatform` wraps another `AiPlatform` config entity reference plus tracing config.

Each plugin is fully self-contained and owns its own config schema.

#### `AiPlatformModelRouter`: plugin type only

```php
interface AiPlatformModelRouterInterface extends ModelRouterInterface, PluginInspectionInterface {}
```

Routing strategy is always specific to a given `AiPlatform` setup, so no standalone config entity is needed. Config lives inside the `AiPlatform` plugin's config blob. Ships with a default `CatalogBasedAiPlatformModelRouter` wrapping Symfony's `CatalogBasedModelRouter`.

***

### Continuity with MR#1214

The config entity + plugin pattern proposed here is the same architecture already established in MR#1214. What has changed is not the pattern but the layer structure: pre-0.8.0, the `AiPlatform` plugin wrapped `PlatformInterface` directly because `ProviderInterface` did not exist yet. Post-0.8.0, the same pattern now covers two layers: `AiPlatformProvider` sits below `AiPlatform`, mapping to the new `ProviderInterface` / `PlatformInterface` split. MR#1214's architecture was not invalidated by #1923, it just gained a layer below it.

***

### Consumer contract

Consumers (agents, chat, search, embeddings) reference an `AiPlatform` config entity by machine name and receive a `PlatformInterface` instance. They never depend on `AiPlatformProvider` or `AiPlatformModelRouter` directly. Factories and factory methods such as `ProviderFactory::create()` and `createPlatform()` are intentionally not surfaced in Drupal. Plugin instances and config entities serve that purpose.

***

### Open questions


#### Should we ship a **Default "skeleton" `AiPlatform` config entity?

The module could ship a default `AiPlatform` config entity backed by a special plugin whose behaviour is to dynamically aggregate all currently configured `AiPlatformProvider` config entities at runtime, with no explicit provider wiring required. This solution is targeting simple sites (Drupal CMS builds) where the added value of the platform abstraction would not be clearly visible, but the complexity would.

Thoughts and feedback welcome.

comments.author: mxr576
comments.body:
On the naming: `AiPlatformProvider` was chosen primarily to avoid collision with 1.x's `AiProvider` plugin type, but the name is awkward precisely because it is defensive rather than descriptive.

An alternative worth considering is the pattern `search_api` uses: placing all plugins under a module-namespaced subdirectory inside `Plugin/`. So instead of `Plugin/AiProvider/`, `Plugin/AiPlatform/`, `Plugin/AiPlatformProvider/`, we would have:

```
Plugin/ai/Provider/    ← 2.x Provider plugins
Plugin/ai/Platform/    ← 2.x Platform plugins
Plugin/ai/ModelRouter/ ← 2.x ModelRouter plugins
```

This lets us use natural names without colliding with the existing `Plugin/AiProvider/` directory from 1.x. Both can coexist in the same codebase during the deprecation window, which matters because we intend to ship a forward compatibility layer in 1.x.

The goal of that forward compat layer is to let contrib module developers start migrating their code against 1.x today, so that when 2.0 ships they can simply drop 1.x support and remove deprecated code. This means the old and new plugin infrastructure must be able to coexist in the same 1.x codebase simultaneously.

That has a direct consequence for manager class naming. The 1.x `\Drupal\ai\AiProviderPluginManager` discovers `Plugin/AiProvider/` and will be marked `@deprecated`. A 2.x-compatible manager discovering `Plugin/ai/Provider/` cannot share the same class name in the same namespace during the 1.x forward compat window. So the 2.x manager needs a distinct name in 1.x, for example `AiPlatformProviderPluginManager`.

This means the defensive naming is not a design flaw but a temporary necessity imposed by the forward compat requirement. In 2.x proper, once all 1.x code is dropped, `AiPlatformProviderPluginManager` could be aliased or renamed back to `AiProviderPluginManager` if desired, giving us the clean name in the end state.

To summarise the proposal:

| | 1.x (current) | 1.x (forward compat layer) | 2.x |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plugin directory | `Plugin/AiProvider/` | `Plugin/ai/Provider/` | `Plugin/ai/Provider/` |
| Plugin manager | `AiProviderPluginManager` `@deprecated` | `AiPlatformProviderPluginManager` | `AiProviderPluginManager` (renamed/aliased) |

The `search_api`-style directories give us clean names where it matters most: the plugin classes that contrib developers write and maintain. The manager class awkwardness during the 1.x window is an acceptable tradeoff.

One more thing worth deciding at the 2.x level: if we adopt the `Plugin/ai/` subdirectory convention for the new Platform and Provider integrations, should we apply it uniformly to all existing plugin types in the module as well, including `AiFunctionCall`, `AiGuardrail`, and others? Doing so would give 2.x a consistent plugin structure throughout. The counterargument is that those plugin types have no naming collision problem, so moving them is purely structural churn with no functional benefit. But a major version is the right moment to make that call, and an inconsistent mix of `Plugin/AiFunctionCall/` and `Plugin/ai/Provider/` in the same module would be confusing for contributors long-term. Worth an explicit decision either way rather than leaving it implicit.

Thoughts?

merge_requests.description:
RegexpGuardrail::processOutput() previously returned PassResult
unconditionally without running the configured regex. This meant
post-generate guardrails using regexp_guardrail never scanned AI

from ChatOutput::getNormalized()->getText() and run the configured
regex pattern against it. Streamed responses (StreamedChatMessageIteratorInterface)
are skipped with a PassResult since their content is not yet available
as a complete string.

Added unit tests covering both processInput() and processOutput() for
matching text, clean text, empty pattern, streamed output, and credit
card pattern detection.

Closes #3580690

merge_requests.title: Draft: #3516754 document current behavior for empty required array props
merge_requests.author: tedbow
merge_requests.description:
### Problem Summary

This issue addresses a **semantic mismatch** in how Canvas treats "required" for `type: array` (multi-cardinality) props. Canvas was incorrectly treating `required: [prop]` as equivalent to `minItems: 1`, which is wrong per JSON Schema. This caused three separate problems:

1.  **UI over-enforced**: The "cannot remove last item" behavior applied to ALL required array props, even those where `value: []` is perfectly schema-valid.
2.  **API under-enforced**: Programmatic saving accepted `value: []` for required array props (correct per spec, but inconsistent with UI behavior). See [MR !923 note\_749762](https://git.drupalcode.org/project/canvas/-/merge_requests/923#note_749762).
3.  **`minItems: 1` was blocked entirely**: Canvas rejected all SDC and code component props that included `minItems`, making it impossible to explicitly opt into ≥1 enforcement.

### Overview

#### Related: \[#3529788\] and \[#3537154\]

\[#3529788\] introduced graceful degradation for required string props (retaining empty string during editing) with a `@todo Expand to support multiple-cardinality`. \[#3537154\] fixed a regression and added a second such `@todo`. Both of those `@todo` items have since been addressed and removed from the codebase, making this issue the remaining piece of the multi-cardinality required-prop story.

#### Root Cause

The false assumption was encoded directly in a code comment in `JsonSchemaType.php`:

> `` // marking an SDC prop as required has the same effect as `minItems: 1` ``

This incorrect belief led Canvas to:

*   Block _all_ `minItems` values in both `JsonSchemaType.php` and `EntityFieldPropSourceMatcher.php` (treating it as redundant with `required`).
*   Call `setRequired(TRUE)` on the Drupal field for ALL required array props — causing the form to show the required asterisk and prevent removing the last item, even when the JSON Schema did not require it.

**This assumption is false.** Proven by tests in [MR !923 note\_749761](https://git.drupalcode.org/project/canvas/-/merge_requests/923#note_749761): with `required: ['data']` but no `minItems`, storing `value: []` produces **zero violations** from `validateComponentInput()`.

#### Key Technical Findings

*   `required: [prop]` in JSON Schema means _"the key must be present in the object"_ — it does NOT mean the array must be non-empty. `value: []` is perfectly schema-valid.
*   `minItems: 1` is the correct JSON Schema mechanism to require ≥1 array item.
*   Drupal Field API's `setRequired(TRUE)` means "≥1 value" — which maps to `minItems: 1` semantics, **not** bare `required`.
*   The UI enforcement chain: PHP `setRequired(TRUE)` → Drupal renders `.form-required` CSS class → JS `isRemoveButtonEnabled()` checks `.form-required` + `rowCount === 1` → remove button disabled.

#### Behavioral Changes

Scenario

Before fix

After fix

SDC with `required: [prop]`, no `minItems`, `value: []`

UI blocks removing last item (incorrect)

UI allows removing all items (correct per JSON Schema)

SDC with `required: [prop]` + `minItems: 1`, `value: []`

Canvas-ineligible (all `minItems` blocked)

Canvas-eligible; `[]` correctly fails validation

SDC with `minItems: 2`

Canvas-ineligible

Still ineligible (Drupal Field API cannot enforce minimum cardinality > 1)

Code component editor: toggle Required ON for array prop

No `minItems` serialized

`minItems: 1` auto-serialized

### Proposed resolution

#### 1 — Allow `minItems: 1` in prop definitions (PHP)

Two files previously blocked ALL `minItems` values:

*   `src/JsonSchemaInterpreter/JsonSchemaType.php` — now allows `minItems` when value is exactly `1`; rejects all other values. Drupal Field API has no concept of minimum cardinality > 1.
*   `src/ShapeMatcher/EntityFieldPropSourceMatcher.php` — same change.

Also removed the false code comment claiming `required` and `minItems: 1` are equivalent.

#### 2 — Code component editor auto-sets `minItems: 1` (TypeScript)

Component authors using the Canvas code editor should not need to know about `minItems: 1` — toggling "Required" on an array prop is enough.

*   `ui/src/types/CodeComponent.ts` — added `minItems?: number` to both `CodeComponentProp` and `CodeComponentPropSerialized`.
*   `ui/src/features/code-editor/codeEditorSlice.ts` — `toggleRequired` now auto-sets `minItems: 1` when enabling required on an array prop, and clears it when disabling.
*   `ui/src/features/code-editor/utils/utils.ts` — `serializeProps` outputs `minItems` and `deserializeProps` reads it back.

#### 3 — Fix UI enforcement (PHP)

`src/Plugin/Canvas/ComponentSource/GeneratedFieldExplicitInputUxComponentSourceBase.php`: `setRequired(TRUE)` is now only called for array props when BOTH `required` AND `minItems: 1` are present. For array props with `required` but no `minItems: 1`, `setRequired(FALSE)` is used — no required asterisk, user CAN remove all items.

No JavaScript changes were needed. `isRemoveButtonEnabled()` already correctly read the `.form-required` CSS class; fixing the PHP source of that class was sufficient.

#### New test SDC: `sparkline_min_1`

`tests/modules/canvas_test_sdc/components/sparkline_min_1/` — a Canvas-eligible sparkline with explicit `minItems: 1`. This forms a three-component test set:

*   `sparkline` — `required: [data]`, no `minItems`; `[]` is schema-valid (Canvas-eligible)
*   `sparkline_min_1` — `required: [data]` + `minItems: 1`; `[]` fails validation (Canvas-eligible)
*   `sparkline_min_2` — `minItems: 2`; Canvas-ineligible

New/updated tests in `SingleDirectoryComponentTest`:

*   `testValidateComponentInputRejectsEmptyRequiredMultiCardinalityPropWithMinItems1`: verifies `value: []` produces ≥1 violations, and `value: [42]` produces 0.
*   `testValidateComponentInputAllowsEmptyRequiredMultiCardinalityPropWithNoMinItems`: clarified that 0 violations for `[]` on a bare-required prop IS correct post-fix behavior, not a bug.

#### Developer experience note

**For SDC authors:** `required: [prop]` alone intentionally does NOT enforce ≥1 items in Canvas. This respects JSON Schema semantics and keeps the SDC valid across non-Canvas contexts. To enforce ≥1 value, add `minItems: 1` explicitly. `minItems > 1` remains unsupported (Drupal Field API cannot enforce it).

**For code component authors:** Toggling "Required" in the Canvas editor for an array prop automatically handles `minItems: 1` — no manual YAML editing needed.

### User interface changes

No new UI visible to content authors. The behavior change — required array props without `minItems: 1` no longer show the required asterisk or block removing the last item — is a bug fix aligning the UI with JSON Schema semantics, not a new UI feature.

merge_requests.title: Resolve #3572553 Correct client props
merge_requests.author: tedbow
merge_requests.description:
This PR removes changes to src/Entity/JavaScriptComponent.php and the corresponding test changes to tests/src/Kernel/Config/JavascriptComponentTest.php that were added in https://git.drupalcode.org/project/canvas/-/merge_requests/567



These changes did two things:

1. Normalized the props from the client. It turns out that was not necessary. I am not sure why they were put in the first place. Perhaps at some point during the MR, the client was sending values in a format where normalization was necessary.  Perhaps they were never needed at all. The way that normalizePropsSchema() was written, it checks if the keys were set at a different level and then moves them. If the client sent them in a way where they are set under "items" as they should be according to the schema, then normalizePropsSchema() would do nothing.



I have stepped through 1.x normalizePropsSchema() when adding a component with a multi-value prop and confirmed normalizePropsSchema() doesn't actually do the normalization because it does not need to.



2. It handles the client sending examples as `[[]]`. I fixed this on the client side in this MR.





Since this MR should effectively have no UI functionality changes, we still need to test that it doesn't mess something up. So, here are the testing instructions from https://git.drupalcode.org/project/canvas/-/merge_requests/567
## Testing Steps: Multi-Value Props

1. Install Drupal using the standard profile and enable the `canvas_dev_mode` module:
   ```
   ddev drush si --account-pass admin -y && ddev drush en canvas_dev_mode -y && ddev drush cr
   ```
2. Open the Canvas editor.
3. Create a code component with a **text** prop and set its cardinality to **limited**, then repeat with **unlimited**.
4. Save the component, reload the page, and confirm the changes persist.
5. Use the **"Add to components"** button to add the component to the page and verify it renders correctly.
6. Repeat steps 3–5 for every prop type that supports multi-value (e.g. integer, number, image, video, link, date, enum).

By: wim leers
By: penyaskito

commits.title: feat(flowdrop_chat): add workflow-based chat processing mode
commits.message:
feat(flowdrop_chat): add workflow-based chat processing mode

Adds a processing_mode setting (legacy | workflow) so chat messages can
optionally be dispatched to a FlowDrop workflow instead of the built-in
PHP pipeline. In workflow mode ChatService delegates execution to
flowdrop_workflow_executor and expects a 'content' output; history is
managed inside the workflow rather than via MemoryManager.

Wires the new settings through the config schema, settings form
(workflow selector with required/visible states), module dependency
on flowdrop_workflow_executor, and DI for the executor, entity type
manager, and logger.

commits.title: feat(flowdrop_chat): add default chat processor workflows
commits.message:
feat(flowdrop_chat): add default chat processor workflows

Ships flowdrop_chat_processor and flowdrop_chat_processor_v2 workflow
definitions that wire GetNodeTypes + BuildSystemPrompt + Chat nodes
into a reusable pipeline for the new workflow-based processing mode.

commits.title: feat(flowdrop_chat): add GetNodeTypes and BuildSystemPrompt node processors
commits.message:
feat(flowdrop_chat): add GetNodeTypes and BuildSystemPrompt node processors

Introduces two FlowDrop node processors so chat message processing can be
modelled as a workflow:
- get_node_types: emits slimmed node type definitions as JSON for LLM
  consumption.
- build_system_prompt: combines node types JSON and workflow state into
  a fully interpolated chat system prompt.

Also ships the matching flowdrop_node_type config entities.

commits.title: feat(flowdrop_chat): extend SystemPromptBuilder with node-types-json API
commits.message:
feat(flowdrop_chat): extend SystemPromptBuilder with node-types-json API

Adds buildWithNodeTypesJson() and getSlimNodeTypesJson() so workflow
nodes can fetch node types separately and interpolate the system prompt
without touching the internal node type manager or history formatting.
Enables a workflow-based chat processing pipeline to reuse the existing
prompt template.

merge_requests.title: Resolve #3575927 "Create drush command"
merge_requests.author: Marcus_Johansson
merge_requests.description:
This issue adds the following Drush tools to the Tool module:

* `drush tool:list` - This lists all the tools available. Can use the `--format=markdown` for coding agents.
* `drush tool:search "search words"` - This searches all the tools available. Can also use the `--format=markdown` for coding agents and `--match=and/or` for matching filter (or by default).
* `drush tool:info tool_data_name` - Gets more detailed information and information how to run a specific tool. Can use the `--format=markdown` for coding agents.
* `drush tool:run tool_data_name` - Execute an agent. Requires `--input` if some input is required. Input can be given as a json encoded payload or one input per property.

There are two added test tools and also kernel tests per each command.

Also very important - when using the tool with a property that is map that is not required and doesn't have a default value, the tool will fail because it tries to set a faulty default value. I have added a fix I think is correct for this at https://git.drupalcode.org/project/tool/-/merge_requests/80/diffs#diff-content-f661d54ffe372c655f1174dab975aacd58cad2d8, however we could create a separate issue for this.

[x] AI Assisted Issue - Parts of the code was written by an AI, but reviewed by a human before creating the MR.
